8957: Archaeology, Politics and Culture
8958: 
8959: I’m puzzled. We’ve almost reached the end of the book, but the most
8960: popular buzzword in theory has hardly been raised yet – ‘postmodernism’.
8961: I’ve heard lots about postmodernism recently, most of it negative,
8962: though no one ever tells me what this means, let alone why it might be
8963: relevant to archaeology.
8964: 
8965: Well, the question of postmodernism is in many ways a red herring. But I
8966: guess I’d better try to deal with it as briefly as possible, though it
8967: may take a whole chapter to do justice to it and all its implications.
8968: Again, I’ll go through the theory before discussing its application to
8969: archaeology.
8970: 
8971: The ‘postmodern condition’ has been defined by the French philosopher
8972: François Lyotard as incredulity toward metanarratives. Lyotard suggests
8973: that the postmodern condition is the condition of knowledge in Western
8974: capitalist societies.
8975: 
8976: A metanarrative is a big story, or a grand claim of absolute truth.
8977: Examples of metanarratives are:
8978: 
8979: 1 A belief in scientific progress, that our ideas of the world are
8980: getting better and better through the use of rational method. In this
8981: sense postmodernists see belief in Science as a metanarrative.
8982: 
8983: 2 Any total scheme of cultural evolution in which societies move from
8984: one stage to another. For example, a belief in a scientific Marxism –
8985: that through an objective science of society we can see grand,
8986: successive stages in human history leading inexorably towards communism.
8987: Or again, a classification of societies into band, tribe, chiefdom and
8988: state.
8989: 
8990: 3 A belief in any absolute system of morality true in all times and in
8991: all places, whether that morality is derived from religious, nationalist
8992: or ethnic claims.
8993: 
8994: 4 Any scheme of ‘progress’ in human history. Such progress may be
8995: aesthetic or artistic: for example, much traditional art history assumes
8996: that artists get better and better at representing Nature. It may be
8997: technological: that humans have more and more ‘complex’ or ‘efficient’
8998: technology through time. Postmodernists argue that assumptions of
8999: ‘progress’ implicitly underline many apparently atheoretical typologies
9000: (for example, pre-Classical to Classical or Gothic to Renaissance in the
9001: history of art and architecture, or most ‘progressive’ archaeological
9002: accounts of ancient technology in which advance succeeds technical
9003: advance).
9004: 
9005: For postmodernists, such beliefs can be traced back to the basic
9006: assumptions of the Enlightenment of the eighteenth century.
9007: Enlightenment philosophers claimed that with the use of Reason, we could
9008: study human affairs in a rational and objective manner. Enlightenment
9009: philosophy therefore implied:
9010: 
9011: (1) Belief in some kind of Utopia. If Reason can be applied to the study
9012: of human affairs, it follows that it can be applied to its organization.
9013: Therefore, we can reach some kind of perfectly organized state, or we
9014: can at least aim for that goal even if it is unattainable within our
9015: lifetime. It can even be a goal that we know we shall never attain but
9016: nevertheless wish to aim for as an ideal, such as perfect ‘scientific’
9017: knowledge of the natural world. Examples are for Marx, communism; for
9018: Fukuyama, bourgeois capitalism; for some physical scientists, a final,
9019: unifying post-Einsteinian theory of the physical universe.
9020: 
9021: (2) A notion of teleology in human affairs. A teleology is an assumption
9022: of an underlying grand process with a definite start point and end
9023: point; for example, within Marxism, the drive towards ever greater
9024: inequality and alienation, culminating in its ultimate overthrow and
9025: dissolution under communism. Cultural evolution tends to be more or less
9026: teleological, though we saw in chapter 9 how recent work in this area
9027: has tended to minimize or deny this teleological element. For the
9028: Enlightenment, a teleological account could be the steady unfolding of
9029: Reason as human history progressed, or variants thereof.
9030: 
9031: (3) A belief in essential properties of a ‘real world’ out there,
9032: separate from language, that we can study independent of the text,
9033: independent of what we write about it. If there is such a real world,
9034: then meanings can be fixed. But for critics of modernity, there is no
9035: such fixity. For Jacques Derrida, ‘il n’y a pas de hors-texte’. The
9036: translation of this phrase can be debated but is generally rendered as
9037: ‘there is nothing outside the text’; there are, instead, a proliferation
9038: of other texts.
9039: 
9040: In other words, the text apparently refers to a ‘real world’, but when
9041: we look at that real world, we find that it too consists of a set of
9042: signifiers that refer to something else. The word ‘pot’ refers to a
9043: definite thing made of clay with handles, but the thing made of clay
9044: with handles itself refers to other things. Any self-evident factual
9045: statement can be pulled apart by its own internal logic, since it cannot
9046: refer to a stable external world beyond the text – the signifiers just
9047: go on and on. Derrida demonstrates this through the technique of
9048: deconstruction, in which he shows that however self-evident and factual
9049: a text appears to be, its meanings can be turned against itself.
9050: 
9051: As a result, it is argued that the postmodern condition is of a world
9052: with fluid, unstable meanings with no final reference point. Instead of
9053: looking for deep, underlying, ‘core’ or ‘essential’ features of a
9054: phenomenon, there is simply endless fluidity and play across surface
9055: meanings.
9056: 
9057: (4) A questioning of disciplinarity. In the eighteenth century, the
9058: study of the ‘real world’ was divided into different disciplines dealing
9059: with different kinds of phenomena. Thus, physics and chemistry deal with
9060: physical processes, whereas biology deals with organisms. But if all
9061: texts refer outwards, if the chain of signifiers just goes on and on
9062: rather than stopping, there is no certainty, no final reference point,
9063: in language – everything just refers to everything else; there is no
9064: final meaning or interpretation. So there can be no distinct
9065: ‘disciplines’ dealing with distinct fields of study. Divides between
9066: disciplines are in this view arbitrary, depending on historical accident
9067: or contingency – how the history of systems of thought happened to
9068: unfold during the Enlightenment.
9069: 
9070: Some postmodern thinkers, for example, have deconstructed the barrier
9071: between history and literature. Since all historical documents are also
9072: literary texts and all historians write narratives, and all literature
9073: exists within a certain historical context, it is argued that there can
9074: be no a priori distinction between history and fiction. Reliance on
9075: factual content, postmodernists argue, is problematic at best: it is
9076: rare to read a novel in which Paris is not the capital of France, or
9077: human beings do not have two arms and two legs. In practice, it is
9078: argued, ‘disciplines’ such as history or literature are constituted and
9079: their boundaries maintained not through ‘real differences’ in their
9080: subject matter, as traditional scholars would claim. Such claimed
9081: differences would owe their reality to an outside world that does not
9082: have any fixed or stable boundaries at all (the ‘logocentric fallacy’).
9083: 
9084: At the end of the twentieth century, the postmodern argument continues,
9085: Western thought entered ‘the postmodern condition’. Historically, all
9086: these grand narratives, certain distinctions and disciplinary boundaries
9087: have fallen away; we don’t have any large stories or certainties left
9088: that we can rely on. The most obvious example given is that of Marxism.
9089: In 1900 Marxists could be confident that world history was on the high
9090: road to revolution and communism. They had a system for explaining the
9091: world which seemed to work and which gave definitive pointers to the way
9092: they should think and act in the present. Now, after the fall of the
9093: Berlin Wall in 1989, things are not so certain, to say the least.
9094: 
9095: Postmodernists spread the attack more widely, however: they also point
9096: to the erosion of confidence in a single ‘scientific method’ discussed
9097: in chapter 3 and also in the decline of Victorian ideas of evolutionary
9098: progress (chapter 9).
9099: 
9100: Postmodernism is a way of thinking that has affected much of Western
9101: thought within and beyond the human sciences. Within architecture, for
9102: example, modernists believed that creation of functional buildings along
9103: ‘rational’ principles (for example, the use of tower blocks) could
9104: contribute to the solution of social problems such as inner-city
9105: crowding and deprivation. The failure of these beliefs, postmodernists
9106: allege, reflects the intellectual failure of underlying modernist
9107: philosophies.
9108: 
9109: To an extent, then, we should talk not so much of postmodernism but of
9110: the postmodern condition: it is alleged that the decline of confidence
9111: in the Enlightenment, in human perfectibility, or to a final and
9112: intrinsic Truth, is not really a proposition to be debated back and
9113: forth. Rather, it is a general state, the way the world is in the
9114: twenty-first century.
9115: 
9116: Postmodern thought often refers to two ‘heroes’. The nineteenth-century
9117: thinker Nietzsche stood against the Enlightenment, arguing against the
9118: sway of Reason. For the twentieth century, they cite the philosopher
9119: Ludwig Wittgenstein. Wittgenstein started his intellectual career as a
9120: logical-positivist philosopher (see chapter 3). He attempted to create a
9121: philosophical language that was perfectly neutral, a language that
9122: described the outside world in completely objective terms. When this
9123: attempt collapsed, Wittgenstein ‘saw the light’ and spent the rest of
9124: his career arguing that communication was simply an endless ‘language
9125: game’ in which the rules were arbitrary but by which we nevertheless
9126: agree to play.
9127: 
9128: I want to pick out one contemporary thinker in particular as important
9129: in the development of postmodern thought, as he is cited more frequently
9130: than anyone else by contemporary archaeologists: Michel Foucault.
9131: 
9132: Foucault looked at many of the concrete institutions of the
9133: Enlightenment, places where Enlightenment values of Reason held sway.
9134: These included the reform of prisons, the replacement of ‘barbaric’
9135: methods of punishment with ‘rational’, ‘enlightened’ penal systems; the
9136: development of modern scientific medicine and clinical practice; the
9137: treatment of madness and the development of the ‘enlightened’ idea that
9138: madness was an illness that could be treated medically. He tried to show
9139: that far from representing ‘progress’ or the rise of more rational or
9140: humane ways of treating human beings, all these institutions were bound
9141: up with new forms of oppression in what he called ‘the disciplinary
9142: society’.
9143: 
9144: Foucault’s second theme was an attack on essentialism. Human beings in
9145: the Enlightenment were assumed to have a set of ‘natural’ or ‘normal’
9146: faculties: the possession of Reason, a certain form of sexuality, desire
9147: for privacy and for individual freedom. In each case Foucault showed how
9148: each historical epoch had its own ideas about what was ‘normal’ and
9149: ‘natural’ for the body to desire or feel. There cannot, therefore, be
9150: reference to a biological base-line for scientists such as ‘the sex
9151: drive’ because there can be no crosscultural definition of such a
9152: base-line.
9153: 
9154: Postmodernism, perhaps more than any other buzz-word or idea in
9155: contemporary theory, acts as a locus of very strong emotional or
9156: visceral responses, both for and against. Most frequently,
9157: ‘postmodernism’ is used as a term of abuse. Many thinkers identify
9158: postmodernism with a denial or deconstruction of any kind of rational
9159: method or empirical enquiry into the past. It is claimed that
9160: postmodernism lays the way open to a disabling relativism, in which if
9161: there is no final truth in the world, all views of the world, or of past
9162: history, are equally valid.
9163: 
9164: It is further alleged that postmodernism leads to a kind of nihilism. If
9165: there is ‘nothing beyond the text’, then the implication, according to
9166: opponents, is that postmodernists view the whole world as ‘just a text’.
9167: Note the inserted ‘just’ or ‘merely’ here, carrying the implication that
9168: words are not important. You will remember that empiricism is, in part,
9169: based on a radical division between ‘words’ and the real world, and the
9170: prioritization of the latter. For many scientists and other empiricists,
9171: postmodernism threatens to become a denial of what they see as the real
9172: world, and of the possibility of rational enquiry.
9173: 
9174: This allegation of nihilism can take a moral turn. If there is ‘nothing
9175: beyond the text’, then there can be no one overarching system of
9176: morality that is true in all times and all places. Postmodernism, then,
9177: is equated with a moral as well as intellectual relativism.
9178: 
9179: For Marxists, some feminists, and other committed to ideas of social
9180: justice, postmodernism is interpreted as holding an additional danger.
9181: It appears to reduce very real issues of equality, deprivation, and even
9182: violence against powerless individuals and groups to the status of a
9183: ‘language game’. Much criticism of postmodernism, then, comes from the
9184: political Left, many of whom see its stress on surface play and irony as
9185: the ideological froth of late capitalism, the last, wildest, most
9186: decadent party before the inevitable collapse back into grim reality. As
9187: I write these words, and the ‘credit crunch’ unfolds and widens into
9188: what is generally agreed to be a profound crisis of global capitalism,
9189: such an analysis seems to gain in strength day by day.
9190: 
9191: Great. What has any of this got to do with archaeology?
9192: 
9193: First, much postprocessual thought clearly shares ground with postmodern
9194: thinking: the loss of confidence in Science, the attack on essentialism,
9195: the stress on a diversity of readings and a lack of fixity of meaning.
9196: 
9197: I would argue against an easy equation with postmodernism, however. The
9198: postprocessual turn in archaeology parallels the postmodern turn in the
9199: human sciences rather than being derived from it. In other words, the
9200: changes that archaeological thought went through in the 1980s and 1990s
9201: were one small example of a wider set of changes across the human
9202: sciences as a whole. This is why I suggested at the start of this
9203: chapter that postmodernism is in some ways a ‘red herring’.
9204: 
9205: Second, the postmodern questioning of disciplinarity has led to interest
9206: in breaking down disciplinary boundaries. At the same time, it also
9207: implies a fragmentation of method. If there is no one Truth ‘out there’
9208: waiting to be discovered any more, it is difficult to see how there can
9209: be one ‘right’ or ‘wrong’ way of doing archaeology.
9210: 
9211: The implication for archaeology is therefore (a) can we really talk
9212: about a single ‘archaeological method’ distinctive from that of other
9213: disciplines? and (b) is there such a thing as a unified method in
9214: archaeology anyway?
9215: 
9216: Third, postmodernism does suggest that archaeologists need to engage
9217: with other forms of knowledge outside the traditionally conceived
9218: purview of ‘archaeology’ or of ‘science’. At the very least, scholars
9219: have to think carefully and seriously about the world outside the
9220: academic and professional practice of archaeology, rather than simply
9221: dismissing other views of the past as a ‘lunatic fringe’. Postmodernism
9222: suggests that if there can be no one neutral method, then other views of
9223: the past can have validity, and not simply be ruled out of court because
9224: they do not conform to established procedures of archaeological method.
9225: 
9226: For example, archaeologists may not agree with the ley line hunters,
9227: Native Americans, other Indigenous peoples, or others who have come to
9228: their version of what the past was like by rules that are not strictly
9229: those of archaeology as an academic discipline – but archaeologists do
9230: have to take them seriously. We cannot simply continue to write about
9231: the past ‘as [we think] it really was’ independently of the present. In
9232: short, we have to look at the social, political and cultural context of
9233: archaeology.
9234: 
9235: Archaeology is Not in a Vacuum
9236: 
9237: Whatever their theoretical differences, most would agree that
9238: archaeological interpretation does not exist in a vacuum, isolated from
9239: the outside world. Before the practising archaeologist goes to work, he
9240: or she reads the morning papers, worries about all the wars in the world
9241: and imminent economic recession. At work, relations of status between
9242: teacher and student are constantly invoked; out on site, decisions about
9243: what to dig and how to dig it are orders from the site director, arrived
9244: at after debate between (usually senior) members of the team or crew. In
9245: short, decisions are taken and archaeology practised within a definite
9246: set of social and political relations whether we like it or not.
9247: 
9248: How, if at all, does this political environment affect archaeological
9249: interpretation? If it does, it does not necessarily do so consciously.
9250: Take, for example, the study of the rise of the Roman Empire. Most
9251: traditional historians of the Roman Empire would angrily, and sincerely,
9252: protest that their accounts of ‘Romanization’ were simply descriptions
9253: of ‘what really happened’, free of any conscious bias. More recent
9254: writers point out that these writers are certainly not consciously
9255: biased, but never see the need to ask the question: why did natives so
9256: rapidly and readily assimilate themselves into an imperial system? This
9257: is at least partly due to the fact that for the previous generation the
9258: benefits of Empire were obvious and self-evident; conversely, for our
9259: generation they need explaining at the very least.
9260: 
9261: So for some, even as archaeology proclaims its neutrality it is deeply
9262: committed:
9263: 
9264: At heart, archaeology is a colonialist endeavour. It is based on, and
9265: generally perpetrates, the values of Western cultures. Privileging the
9266: material over the spiritual and the scientific over the religious,
9267: archaeological practice is solidly grounded in Western ways of knowing
9268: the world. (Smith and Wobst 2005a: 5)
9269: 
9270: Archaeology, as cultural practice, is always a politics, always a
9271: morality. (Shanks and Tilley 1987: 212)
9272: 
9273: Politics as seen by these thinkers is more than ‘party politics’. It is
9274: about power in the broadest sense, from the policies and practices of
9275: the State down to the smallest human interaction. In this view,
9276: everything that we say and do is political in some sense. When a site
9277: director gives instructions to his or her team, when we write that an
9278: argument is ‘strong’ or ‘weak’, when we choose which site projects to
9279: fund and which to ignore, when a self-confident student cuts across a
9280: more diffident and nervous student in debate – these are all political
9281: decisions and actions.
9282: 
9283: This point is an emotive and controversial one. Many students have told
9284: me that they dislike politics, and specifically chose the study of
9285: archaeology to get away from it. The Classical author Livy wrote his
9286: History with this aim in mind also. But we have seen many times in this
9287: book how we always return to the present in the end, whatever
9288: philosophical viewpoint we take. In chapter 2 we noted that the data are
9289: mute, that the past does not exist; rather, statements about it are made
9290: in the here and now. In chapter 6 we discussed the concept of ideology,
9291: and earlier in chapter 5 we saw early versions of systems theory
9292: condemned by critical theorists as just such an ideology. Chapter 8 saw
9293: feminists criticizing archaeology and academic discourse in general for
9294: its phallocentrism.
9295: 
9296: The common point of all these critiques is very simple: when an
9297: archaeologist claims that their position is neutral and apolitical, what
9298: are they trying to hide?
9299: 
9300: Case Study: African Burial Ground
9301: 
9302: During the eighteenth century, between 10,000 and 20,000 people of
9303: African descent were buried in an area of New York City that came to be
9304: known as the African Burial Ground. In one estimate, ‘at least half of
9305: the present African-descent population in the United States probably has
9306: at least one ancestor buried in this area’. The area fell out of use
9307: after 1795, and the land was filled in and overbuilt. The role of slaves
9308: of African descent in eighteenth- century New York was gradually
9309: forgotten. According to Michael Blakey:
9310: 
9311: The very existence of an African Burial Ground in colonial New York
9312: raised the issue of false historical representation. The vast majority
9313: of educated Americans had learned that there was little if any African
9314: presence in New York during the colonial period, and that the northern
9315: American colonies had not engaged in the practice of slavery. The Burial
9316: Ground helped show that these notions comprised a kind of national myth.
9317: (Blakey 1995: 546)
9318: 
9319: In 1991 the burial ground was ‘rediscovered’ and partly excavated by
9320: archaeologists in advance of redevelopment. Over 400 burials were
9321: excavated before public concern led to legislation to suspend excavation
9322: and to protect and display the site.
9323: 
9324: This concern had come from members of the African-American community in
9325: New York. The nature of this concern and the way it affected
9326: archaeological practice is revealing. In the first place, formal
9327: lawsuits arguing that the site should be preserved found little comfort
9328: in existing legislation, which had been framed around a narrow view of
9329: ‘archaeological significance’ rather than the cultural associations a
9330: site might have for specific ethnic groups. Such lawsuits were however
9331: backed up by the support of black activist groups, Mayor Dinkins and the
9332: Congressional Black Caucus. At one point, a one-day blockade of the site
9333: halted redevelopment work.
9334: 
9335: Figure 12.1 A coffin from the African Burial Ground, New York. The heart
9336: shape is made of tacks; it has been interpreted as a west African symbol
9337: 
9338: [c12_image001.jpg]
9339: 
9340: Second, the archaeological research was transformed. Initial work on the
9341: bones was done by the Metropolitan Forensic Anthropology Team (MFAT),
9342: but public pressure ensured that in late 1993 the bones were transferred
9343: to the Cobb Biological Anthropology Laboratory at Howard University.
9344: 
9345: The analysis of the bones was not simply done by a different team: it
9346: had a different research design that linked together political concerns
9347: (who controls these remains?) with new intellectual ideas about
9348: ethnicity, of the sort discussed in chapter 8, and new techniques of
9349: archaeological practice.
9350: 
9351: Not only was the research design changed; the Howard University team
9352: publicized the research design and offered it up to criticism and
9353: modification from the descendant black community. Even the name of the
9354: place and the project became a contested topic. The site had been
9355: referred to as ‘The Negro’s Burial Ground’ before objections coupled
9356: with academic arguments led to it being renamed the ‘African Burial
9357: Ground’. In 1993 the burial ground was designated a National Historic
9358: Landmark and plans for redevelopment were scrapped. In 2006, the site
9359: was designated a National Monument, and a memorial and grounds have been
9360: laid out on the site (figure 12.1).
9361: 
9362: The results of archaeological analysis paint a compelling portrait of
9363: the exploitation and violence suffered by enslaved Africans, and equally
9364: to the active resistance of people of African descent to this
9365: exploitation and violence. Most of the dead were buried in coffins and
9366: shrouds, and oriented east-west, like white New Yorkers; but the
9367: presence of beads and shells point towards continuing African
9368: traditions.
9369: 
9370: The major implication for the practice of archaeology is that while what
9371: we do is unavoidably political, changing the way we do it is far from
9372: straightforward. In this case, it was not simply a matter of rewriting a
9373: research design, but of opening up that design to scrutiny from
9374: non-archaeologists – people who themselves occupy a problematic position
9375: within their own communities. There was no one ‘right’ or ‘wrong’ way to
9376: handle the African Burial Ground controversy, though there were better
9377: and worse ways. To a great extent, sympathetic and sensitive handling of
9378: the archaeological interpretation depends on the acknowledgement that
9379: there is a multiplicity of different views.
9380: 
9381: Indigenous Archaeologies
9382: 
9383: Many archaeologists have come to recognize that archaeology is based on,
9384: and generally reflects, the values of Western cultures. In privileging
9385: the material, scientific, observable world over the spiritual,
9386: experiential, and unquantifiable aspects of archaeological sites,
9387: ancient peoples, and artifacts, archaeological practice demonstrates
9388: that it is solidly grounded in Western ways of categorising, knowing,
9389: and interpreting the world (Atalay 2006: 280).
9390: 
9391: One of the key developments in the development of archaeology in the
9392: last 20 years has been the growing engagement with archaeology by
9393: Indigenous peoples. ‘Indigenous’ is a difficult term to define, and is
9394: often spelt with a capital I to denote its complexity, but generally
9395: refers to ‘native’ peoples who have been dispossessed in colonial
9396: contexts, such as Native Americans or the Indigenous peoples of
9397: Australia.
9398: 
9399: A specific locus of disagreement has been the collections and storing of
9400: cultural artefacts and human remains. Huge quantities of archaeological
9401: and ethnographic material were collected in the nineteenth and twentieth
9402: centuries by Western scholars from around the world, as any wander round
9403: the store-rooms of many museums will indicate. The motivation for this
9404: collection varied, but was often done within a framework of unilineal
9405: cultural evolution, that saw the collection and classification of this
9406: cultural material as a cornerstone of evolutionary and scientific study.
9407: For many ‘native’ peoples, however, these actions are more accurately
9408: seen as acts of plunder, the cultural counterpart to the processes of
9409: colonialism and in many contexts cultural and physical genocide that
9410: accompanied the colonial process.
9411: 
9412: For many Indigenous peoples, cultural tradition unites present and past,
9413: and the Western dichotomy between present and past (as visualized for
9414: example in figure 2.1) is rejected. Within such a world-view, cultural
9415: objects are more than simply ‘artefacts’ belonging to some distant past,
9416: and are certainly not commodities that can be alienated. Objects are
9417: sacred and have spiritual power. The attribution of a profound cultural
9418: value is especially true of human remains.
9419: 
9420: Archaeology has engaged with these issues in different ways. First, the
9421: importance of genuinely collaborative research and engagement with ‘user
9422: communities’ has been stressed. In the past, archaeology was often
9423: justified exclusively in terms of ‘science’ or ‘pure research’ –
9424: knowledge about the past for its own sake. Increasingly such a
9425: justification is seen as not good enough, on the grounds argued above,
9426: namely that knowledge is never pure and disinterested in nature, and
9427: when such a claim is made (‘I’m not interested in politics, I just want
9428: to collect and study old artefacts in peace’) it usually masks the real
9429: power relations at work. As with the African Burial Ground, it is argued
9430: that archaeologists need to take seriously the views of Indigenous and
9431: other local communities, and to collaborate in the framing of research,
9432: rather than merely treating those outside the archaeological community
9433: as an ‘audience’.
9434: 
9435: Second, an increasing (though still small) number of Indigenous people
9436: are now trained and work as archaeologists. Many of these scholars are
9437: working actively to construct new kinds of archaeology – ‘Indigenous
9438: archaeologies’ – that move beyond what are seen as colonial practices.
9439: Indigenous archaeologies are by their very nature a varied and
9440: developing set of practices. They draw, in part, on postprocessual
9441: critiques, on many of the issues of identity and interpretation
9442: discussed in chapter 8, and on postcolonial thinking in the human
9443: sciences. Indigenous scholars acknowledge the irony in this situation:
9444: ‘we must sometimes use the master’s tools … to create a
9445: counter-discourse to Western approaches that have consistently worked to
9446: destroy or silence our Indigenous ways of knowing’ (Atalay 2006: 296).
9447: 
9448: Third, political legislation has changed the relationship between
9449: archaeologists and Indigenous peoples in different countries. As the
9450: rights of Indigenous peoples have been increasingly asserted, their
9451: claims over cultural material and human remains have been legislated for
9452: in different ways. The most notable example is NAGPRA (North American
9453: Graves Protection and Repatriation Act), passed in 1990. NAGPRA
9454: specified that most federally-funded institutions such as museums and
9455: universities draw up inventories of their collections and send summaries
9456: of all Native American human remains, sacred and ceremonial objects, and
9457: other objects deemed to be of ‘cultural patrimony’ to tribes likely to
9458: be associated with such material. What NAGPRA meant, then, was that much
9459: of North American archaeological practice changed radically after this
9460: date, with issues of ‘Indigenous knowledge’ and relations with
9461: Indigenous groups gaining a much higher profile than before, and the
9462: notion of the archaeologist as detached scientist being seriously
9463: eroded.
9464: 
9465: A classic example of the conflict between ‘Western’ and Indigenous
9466: cultural values is the dispute over ‘Kennewick Man’. In 1996, human
9467: skeletal remains were found on the bank of the Columbia River at
9468: Kennewick, in Washington State in the northwestern USA. These remains
9469: were carbon-14 dated to over 8000 years old. According to some
9470: academics, the remains showed ‘Caucasoid’ traits, and Kennewick Man
9471: became a controversial pawn in the context of a debate over the single
9472: or multiple nature of prehistoric migration to the Americas.
9473: 
9474: For local Umatilla and other Native American groups seeking ownership of
9475: the remains, the scientifically determined date of the skeleton was
9476: irrelevant, since their religious beliefs told them that their people
9477: had been present since the dawn of time. They requested custody of the
9478: remains, with the intention of reburying them. The Native American claim
9479: was rejected in a 2004 court judgment, as the court determined that
9480: ‘kinship’ between the modern Umatilla and Kennewick Man could not be
9481: ‘proven’. The remains have been extensively studied by scientists, and
9482: are now at the Burke Museum in Washington.
9483: 
9484: The conclusion of the Kennewick Man case points up a key issue in the
9485: future development of Indigenous archaeologies: the definition of the
9486: ‘descendant community’. It is easy to assert Indigenous rights, but in
9487: practice, the question of who are the descendants of the Indigenous
9488: community is not always an easy one. In the past, archaeologists often
9489: tended to ignore or write out of their interpretations any reference to
9490: descendant groups. For example, the use of the term ‘Anasazi’ (meaning
9491: ‘the vanished ones’) to refer to the builders and inhabitants of Chaco
9492: Canyon and the adjacent region in the American Southwest tended to
9493: downplay links between these sites and descendant Hopi groups.
9494: Conversely, the replacement of the term Anasazi with Ancestral Pueblo
9495: acknowledges and affirms these links.
9496: 
9497: However, different cultural groups often have competing claims to
9498: territory and to cultural resources. If archaeology is not a neutral
9499: science, then it follows that the role of archaeology in adjudicating
9500: these claims cannot be a simple or straightforward one. The classic case
9501: of such competing claims is the site of Ayodha, in northern India, held
9502: by Hindus to be the birthplace of the god Rama. Until 1992 the alleged
9503: site of Rama’s birth was occupied by a Muslim mosque, itself five
9504: centuries old. This mosque was destroyed by Hindu nationalists in 1992;
9505: over 2000 people were killed in associated riots. After the destruction,
9506: archaeological excavations claimed to ‘prove’ that, stratified below the
9507: mosque, were the remains of an earlier Hindu temple. Many, if not most,
9508: archaeologists would wish to distance themselves from one-sided claims
9509: of exclusive ownership of the Ayodha site, and to be very wary of being
9510: drawn into uncritical support for a single set of claims; in this case,
9511: then, a simple assertion of the rights of a single cultural group, or
9512: indeed an assertion that archaeologists should simply describe and
9513: record objectively what was found, is simply not enough on its own.
9514: 
9515: Multiculturalism, Diversity and Inclusion
9516: 
9517: Three theoretical buzz-words have come to dominate much of the cultural
9518: and political context of archaeological work: multiculturalism,
9519: diversity, and inclusion. All are strongly related to the theoretical
9520: agendas laid out in Chapter 8, and all are theoretical concepts whose
9521: impact on archaeology has occurred in part through their application
9522: through legislation – they are probably familiar to the student from
9523: contemporary cultural discourse. I will define each in turn and explain
9524: something of their relevance to archaeology.
9525: 
9526: Multiculturalism stresses the importance of different cultures, races
9527: and ethnicities within a single population or nation-state. It tends to
9528: celebrate different cultural practices, to see this difference as
9529: enriching rather than threatening within a society, and to resist the
9530: idea that nations or populations can or should attempt to ‘assimilate’
9531: different groups to a single culture. Diversity refers not simply to
9532: cultural difference, but to other dimensions of difference also – to
9533: gender, class, family circumstance, and disability. Archaeological and
9534: other organizations are required to acknowledge and respect diversity,
9535: for example in the workforce. Inclusion is about making diversity work
9536: in practice – of taking the necessary steps to ensure that people of
9537: different backgrounds are given the same opportunities, and that
9538: employers and institutions do not simply assume that all their staff or
9539: participants are of a particular cultural background (for example,
9540: ensuring that working hours can respect a variety of religious
9541: practices, or proactively ensuring and extending access for people with
9542: disabilities).
9543: 
9544: Archaeologists have engaged with all these themes in a variety of ways –
9545: as with NAGPRA, legislation has compelled institutions to comply whether
9546: their senior staff were personally in sympathy with such agendas or not.
9547: We have already seen how it was not good enough for archaeologists to
9548: sit back and ‘objectively’ study the remains from the African Burial
9549: Ground – it was crucial to explore ways of actively engaging with
9550: descendant and other communities. Government legislation to promote
9551: agendas of diversity, multiculturalism and inclusion have been rolled
9552: out, particularly in Britain and the European Community. Consequently,
9553: the key priorities of heritage bodies and museums have been transformed.
9554: 
9555: Most crucially, in many contexts it is now necessary for archaeologists
9556: to engage actively with these issues rather than simply sit back and
9557: take an intellectual pose. In Britain, for example, there has been a
9558: welcome shift in the last decade away from what I have termed a national
9559: ‘culture of complacency’:
9560: 
9561: No longer could universities shrug their shoulders and claim that they
9562: would admit more students from underprivileged backgrounds if they made
9563: the effort to apply; it was now incumbent upon them to show that they
9564: had an active policy of encouraging recruitment and were monitoring its
9565: effectiveness. No longer could established heritage bodies like museums,
9566: English Heritage, and the National Trust shrug their shoulders and say
9567: that they would welcome more ethnic minority visitors if they only came
9568: through the doors; relevant policies to attract such visitors and make
9569: monuments and displays relevant to them had to be shown to exist.
9570: (Johnson 2006: 166)
9571: 
9572: Ironically, then, the values of an engaged and committed archaeology
9573: have been promoted in many countries not simply through the activities
9574: of some archaeologists, but also through the necessities of complying
9575: with Government legislation. They have also, in many cases, been thought
9576: out and promoted through museums and ‘heritage’ agencies more widely,
9577: deeply and effectively than they have through Universities.
9578: Consequently, much of the most current, original and radical thinking on
9579: the engagement of archaeology with social justice issues has come not
9580: from academics, but from those working in ‘heritage management’,
9581: ‘community’ or ‘public’ archaeology however defined.
9582: 
9583: The Relativism Question
9584: 
9585: Well, it seems to me we’ve reached an impasse. There is no future in
9586: ideas of academic objectivity. We’ve seen that all versions of the past
9587: are political in some sense. So it seems we just make up whatever
9588: stories suit us, are convenient in the political present.
9589: 
9590: No, that will not do. There are better and worse interpretations of the
9591: past. I do not know of a single archaeologist of whatever theoretical
9592: stripe who would disagree. Even Jacques Derrida writes that ‘[the
9593: relativist] definition of deconstruction is false (that’s right: false,
9594: not true) and feeble; it supposes a bad (that’s right: bad, not good)
9595: and feeble reading of numerous texts, first of all mine’ (1988: 136–7).
9596: 
9597: The criticisms of an unrestrained relativism are many, various and
9598: irrefutable. It is demonstrably wrong: archaeology has ‘progressed’, we
9599: no longer believe in the Creation of 4004 BC. Relativism involves a
9600: logical fallacy: ‘all views are of equal value – including this one’.
9601: Relativism undercuts the value of archaeology as a form of knowledge
9602: about the past, and if pursued to its logical implication would do
9603: archaeologists out of a job. For many, the most fundamental and damning
9604: criticism of relativism is that it destroys our ability to speak out
9605: against politically undesirable and even obnoxious views of the past,
9606: the classic example being Holocaust denial groups. Archaeologists might
9607: wish to engage with Indigenous groups at a variety of levels, and
9608: acknowledge the validity of different views at the African Burial Ground
9609: – but who would suggest that this means we have to acknowledge racist
9610: views as of equal value?
9611: 
9612: Modern theorists take several roads out of this impasse. Many continue
9613: to insist that most archaeological facts and interpretations are
9614: mundane, beyond politics. Politics does play a part in this view, but at
9615: a level of theory that happens ‘after’ primary interpretation – we can
9616: collect our pottery sherds, and worry about the politics when it comes
9617: to interpreting them. This is a position implicitly taken by many
9618: traditional or culture-historical archaeologists.
9619: 
9620: Alternatively, one can stress a positivist view of scientific method as
9621: a way to avoid the relativist trap. Remember that positivists stress the
9622: importance of separating the context of discovering or suggesting an
9623: idea from the context of its testing and evaluation. A processualist
9624: might well write: ‘Of course we are influenced by contemporary ideas. It
9625: couldn’t be any other way – our hypotheses about the past processes, by
9626: definition, are framed in the present. But the essence of science is to
9627: use rigorous procedures of testing to move beyond this, through
9628: observation of the archaeological record. This is why the development of
9629: middle-range theory is so important. Only by developing robust ways of
9630: testing our ideas against archaeological material can we move beyond
9631: simply telling stories about the past.
9632: 
9633: ‘For a scientist, you can get an idea about how atoms might work, or
9634: about physical processes, from the newspaper, from a conversation, or
9635: from a drugcrazed hallucination. It doesn’t matter where the idea comes
9636: from. What does matter is that you then test that idea. That is, you set
9637: up that idea as a rigorous hypothesis and then test it against material
9638: that you observe. That is the way science works, and it is the way it
9639: has been proven to work.’
9640: 
9641: Thus, for example, Jerry Sabloff is quite happy to note Wilk’s work
9642: relating different explanations of the Classic Maya collapse to
9643: contemporary political events, but takes care to suggest how this can
9644: help us do better science:
9645: 
9646: While Wilk’s correlations are interesting, they do not prove that
9647: current intellectual trends determine which views of the collapse became
9648: popular, nor is there anything necessarily ‘wrong’ about archaeologists
9649: letting political trends influence their thinking. However, if his
9650: correlations are valid, they indicate how careful scholars must be to
9651: ensure that current biases are not blinding them to alternative
9652: hypotheses or limiting the kinds of data they collect. (Sabloff 1990:
9653: 166–7)
9654: 
9655: In other words, such a view will grant that everything we say is in some
9656: sense political, but by using science we can and should move away from
9657: just telling stories.
9658: 
9659: There has been a series of discussions attempting to steer a middle
9660: course between positivism and relativism. Bruce Trigger has suggested
9661: that the course of archaeology is driven both by political context and
9662: by a progressively better understanding of the data. He advocates a
9663: modified version of middlerange theory to grasp the interplay of
9664: different kinds of influence. Alison Wylie suggests that the way we
9665: relate theory to data will vary according to the context or locality we
9666: are working in:
9667: 
9668: The question of what epistemic stance is appropriate … should be settled
9669: locally, in the light of what we have come to know about specific
9670: subject matters and about the resources we have for their investigation.
9671: We should resist the pressure to adopt a general epistemic stance
9672: appropriate to all knowledge claims. (Wylie 1992b: 35)
9673: 
9674: In my view, the definitive discussion of this problem is Elizabeth
9675: Brumfiel’s analysis of the quality of textiles in Aztec Mexico. In this
9676: period, women under Aztec rule had to produce tribute cloth; Brumfiel’s
9677: feminist perspective made her sceptical that the women were
9678: ideologically ‘duped’ and led her to expect that through time ‘the
9679: quality of tribute cloth was deteriorating … because Indian women were
9680: engaged in resistance. Indian women were deliberately turning out
9681: undersized, loosely woven cloth with thick, loosely spun thread as a
9682: form of opposition to tribute extraction’.
9683: 
9684: However, the archaeological evidence suggested that quality did not
9685: decline. ‘Rejecting the hypothesis’, however, ‘did not lead [Brumfiel]
9686: to reject the entire framework of theoretical propositions from which it
9687: was derived’; rather she created an ad hoc modification of her theory:
9688: ‘resistance to exploitation does not occur when activities … are
9689: vulnerable to supervision and control by the dominant class. This
9690: enabled me to continue to affirm that proponents of ideological
9691: domination have underestimated the frequency of resistance and the
9692: importance of coercion in maintaining dominance’ (Brumfiel 1996: 454,
9693: 458).
9694: 
9695: Brumfiel concludes that:
9696: 
9697: Long before the choice between coercion and ideological domination is
9698: decided by appeals to data, the need to choose will be eliminated by a
9699: theoretical insight that clarifies the complementary relationship
9700: between the two phenomena. Even so, data will have an important role to
9701: play. … For example, these observations on the quality of tribute cloth
9702: production … have opened a new line of enquiry for me concerning how the
9703: vulnerability of men and women to state coercion may differ because of
9704: differences in the location of their work or in the kind of goods that
9705: they produce for the state. The knowledge I have gained from this
9706: enquiry will affect the way I regard ideological domination and
9707: resistance in future studies. (Brumfiel 1996: 459)
9708: 
9709: My own view is largely in agreement with Brumfiel; indeed, I know of no
9710: archaeologist who would, either openly or implicitly, declare themselves
9711: to be an unqualified relativist. In fact, peace has broken out in the
9712: realm of epistemology. With the notable exceptions of hard-line
9713: positivists and behavioural archaeologists, few would disagree that
9714: archaeologists are unavoidably influenced by our social and political
9715: circumstances, and that ‘raw data’ do not exist in any unproblematic or
9716: unbiased way. Conversely, few would disagree that the data are
9717: important, and that at the very least they form a network of resistance
9718: to the interpretations we wish to put on them. Most of us would say this
9719: network of resistance is very strong, and that our methods should try to
9720: strengthen it.
9721: 
9722: Discussions over the perils of relativism are, I suspect, far more to do
9723: with the perceptions, fears and uncertainties of the contemporary world,
9724: in particular the uncertain place of academic and intellectual practice
9725: in its wider context. Rather than replies to or refutations of a
9726: postmodernist philosophy, they are symptomatic of the difficulties of
9727: working within a very challenging set of cultural circumstances within
9728: which archaeology is unavoidably engaged. All the contributors to this
9729: debate have seen through the naïve certainties of positivist rhetoric
9730: with its definite and simplistic divide between data and theory to the
9731: realities of a world that is uncertain and difficult to live and work
9732: in.
9733: 
9734: 13
9735: 
9736: Conclusion: The Future of Theory
9737: 
9738: Now just hold on a minute. There’s one huge omission from the entirety
9739: of the preceding text that can’t be permitted to go on any longer. What
9740: exactly is theory? You can’t get away with not defining theory for the
9741: entire book.
9742: 
9743: Well, we saw in chapter 1 that there were different definitions of what
9744: theory is. I provisionally defined theory as ‘the order we put facts
9745: in’, and also made reference to why we do archaeology, and to ‘issues of
9746: interpretation’. As we have seen in the course of this book, however,
9747: there are different theoretical views of what facts are and how they
9748: relate to theory, different rationales or ideologies behind
9749: archaeological practice, and very different views of what constitutes
9750: ‘interpretation’.
9751: 
9752: Remember figure 4.1. For many positivists, theory is a definable set of
9753: propositions that can be set up and tested against data. For advocates
9754: of middle-range theory, archaeologists have been developing a set of
9755: methods over the last 30 years that enable us to test theory against
9756: data.
9757: 
9758: In this view, theory is defined quite narrowly and precisely. It is
9759: simply a set of general propositions. These can be either
9760: generalizations about the archaeological record (‘the rise of early
9761: states correlates with the emergence of redistributive trade networks’)
9762: or about how we should do archaeology (‘we must make testability the
9763: central criterion of our epistemology’). The data that archaeologists
9764: manipulate cannot be changed in this view: data just exist independently
9765: of the theory we are using. Many of the things we do as archaeologists
9766: (site catchment analysis, random sampling, retrieval of surface scatters
9767: of pottery) are in this view simply techniques. The questions the
9768: techniques are designed to answer may be theoretical, but the techniques
9769: and the data are outside the purview of theory.
9770: 
9771: At the other extreme, all archaeology is theoretical; theory is defined
9772: very broadly. As we have seen, postprocessualists have argued that both
9773: techniques and data are ‘theory-laden’, and that we must theorize
9774: ‘interpretation at the trowel’s edge’. Many would go further and,
9775: following critical theory, argue that site catchment analysis and other
9776: techniques masquerade as neutral but are actually deeply theoretical.
9777: Remember that for poststructuralists even a banal factual statement like
9778: ‘there are 23 pig molars from Layer 346’ is a statement within a text
9779: that can be deconstructed. Or they might take a different angle: ‘Of
9780: course the number of pig molars is a factual statement but why does it
9781: appear where it does in this text? Why did the site director decide to
9782: put so much emphasis on economic reconstruction at this site to the
9783: exclusion of all others? In short, the question may be ‘factual’, but we
9784: want to question the question.’
9785: 
9786: So, just as feminists argue that inequality penetrates deeply into
9787: everyday life, and just as many would draw a broad and deep view of the
9788: political engagement of archaeology, so in the interpretive view all or
9789: almost all of archaeological practice is drawn within the remit of
9790: theory. Indeed, one of the key developments in the last decade has been
9791: the theoretical questioning and exploration of field practice (see
9792: below).
9793: 
9794: How one defines theory, then, depends on one’s own theoretical
9795: viewpoint. It would be wrong for a book written as an introduction to
9796: different theoretical views, that claims to be sympathetic or at least
9797: fair to a diversity of views, to nevertheless insist on one definition
9798: of theory to the exclusion of others. My preferred definition is ‘the
9799: order you put facts in’, in part because this is a short and accessible
9800: definition, in part because this seems to me to be vague in the right
9801: places – it allows different theoretical viewpoints to coexist within
9802: such a definition (though not all, admittedly). More importantly and
9803: fundamentally, it draws attention to the way that data and theory are
9804: dialectically related. In other words, ‘data’ and ‘theory’ have to be
9805: understood as part of a larger whole in which the nature of one cannot
9806: be understood without the other.
9807: 
9808: Where We Are Now
9809: 
9810: In 2009, what is the current state of archaeological theory? Such a
9811: superficially simple question is very difficult to answer. One of the
9812: striking things about the responses to the first, 1999 edition of this
9813: book was that they differed very sharply, not just in their evaluation
9814: of which strands or movements in archaeology were or were not
9815: productive, but more fundamentally – in their descriptive assessment of
9816: what was or was not ‘central’ or ‘marginal’, ‘serious’ or ‘frivolous’ in
9817: archaeological theory. ‘I don’t know why you waste time in your book
9818: discussing position X; no serious scholar believes that now’, was a
9819: frequent comment from a fervent believer in position Y; one week later,
9820: at another institution, the comment would be ‘I don’t know why you waste
9821: time discussing position Y; everyone settled on position X a long time
9822: ago’. The mental alarm bells of any scholar should ring loudly every
9823: time a different view is not engaged with, but rather summarily
9824: dismissed on an a priori basis, rejected as a ‘basic misunderstanding’,
9825: or ruled ‘out of court’ in this way.
9826: 
9827: One critical difference in the perception of where we are now remains
9828: the different institutional position of academic archaeology in
9829: different countries. I have commented in earlier chapters on the
9830: position of much North American academic archaeology within Anthropology
9831: departments, versus the European tradition either of separate
9832: departments or of closer alliances with Classics and History. In the
9833: last decade, this contrast has had two important consequences.
9834: 
9835: The first is a difference in the impact of postmodernism and what has
9836: been held to be its implications. In North America from the 1990s
9837: onwards, postmodernism had a huge impact on cultural anthropology in
9838: particular. At the same time, there was a wider cultural context of a
9839: ‘war on science’ in which, culturally and politically, it seemed that
9840: science was both being politicized and its integrity attacked by
9841: Creationists and others. It seemed, then, to many North American
9842: archaeologists that the themes they held dear as essential to a
9843: responsible and rigorous archaeology were being seriously eroded. In
9844: retrospect, it seems to me that many of the more extreme and negative
9845: reactions to the early polemics of British postprocessual archaeology
9846: were in part displaced reactions to what was going on closer to home,
9847: across the corridor in Anthropology departments, and across the road in
9848: City Halls and in the White House.
9849: 
9850: The second consequence is a differently constructed relationship with
9851: Indigenous peoples. In North America, and also in other contexts around
9852: the world such as Australia, there has been in the past a very sharp
9853: dividing line between how ‘archaeologists’ construct the past, and
9854: beliefs in the past held by Indigenous peoples. For many, there is a
9855: binary opposition between these two world-views, which often appear at
9856: first sight to be quite incommensurate. Kennewick Man, discussed in the
9857: last chapter, is a case in point.
9858: 
9859: For Europeans, the relationship between archaeology and Indigenous
9860: peoples is constructed in a different way. First, with arguable
9861: exceptions such as the Saami, there are few clearly Indigenous goups,
9862: and no obvious or straightforward definition of what an Indigenous
9863: people is. For example, British people of Anglo-Saxon descent can be
9864: accused by the Welsh of having driven out the ‘native British’ from much
9865: of England in the centuries after ad 400. Second, cultural claims of
9866: descent and ownership of territory do not always reflect the aspects of
9867: a struggle of the dispossessed, but rather take on a nationalist and
9868: politically unpleasant tinge, for example in arguments over who has
9869: rights to what land in the former Yugoslavia.
9870: 
9871: Making a judgment on what is important in theory today is rendered
9872: especially difficult by the great diversity of contexts in which
9873: ‘theory’ operates. I have argued that all archaeology is theoretical,
9874: but overt discussions of theory are constructed or inflected in
9875: different ways between and within communities of archaeologists – for
9876: example, between different regional or national traditions, between
9877: different area/period specialisms, or between archaeologists working in
9878: different contexts (academic versus ‘professional’).
9879: 
9880: The Fall and Rise of Empiricism
9881: 
9882: One way to look at the question of where we are now is to put it the
9883: question in very crude terms – to ask: what, in practice, is the
9884: theoretical attitude adopted by most archaeologists, ‘professional’ as
9885: well as academic, around the whole world? I suspect that the answer
9886: would have to be that it remains a crude and unreflective empiricism, in
9887: the sense that the data are held to speak for themselves, without the
9888: need for intervening theory. Empiricism in this sense can to some extent
9889: be seen as lurking behind a number of varied positions and statements
9890: about theory that often sound quite obvious and inoffensive in
9891: themselves until their implications are teased out, for example:
9892: 
9893: -   that only academics have the time for theory, and ‘professional’
9894:     archaeologists, or those working in museums or cultural resource
9895:     management, have ‘no time’ to do it;
9896: -   that as an archaeologist one can choose between taking or not taking
9897:     a theoretical approach; and/or that some approaches are ‘very
9898:     theoretical’ while others are ‘very empirical’;
9899: -   that the moment for ‘doing theory’ occurs after ‘a basic grasp of
9900:     the data’ (a moment that never seems to be ‘now’);
9901: -   a rhetoric which implies that ‘theory’ is somehow less empirically
9902:     grounded than archaeology without explicit theory; this rhetoric
9903:     usually comes with an implicit reliance on ‘common sense’;
9904: -   an implication that ‘plain speaking’ is superior to a theoretical
9905:     ‘rhetoric’ (an assertion which, paradoxically, is itself a classic
9906:     piece of rhetoric);
9907: -   the notion that ‘more empirical’ studies will be of more ‘enduring
9908:     value’, whereas theories are merely ‘passing fads’;
9909: -   the definition, on criteria that are taken as commonsensical and not
9910:     in need of explication, of questions which are more knowable or
9911:     legitimate than others; for example, that ‘subsistence’ or
9912:     ‘lordship’ is a legitimate explanation for patterns in the landscape
9913:     whereas exploration of memory or gender is ‘wild’ or ‘fanciful’; and
9914: -   at the most basic level, the notion that the accumulation of more
9915:     data, in and of itself, will automatically lead to a better
9916:     knowledge of the past.
9917: 
9918: All these positions make a false assumption. They imply that while
9919: theory may or may not be a ‘legitimate’ part of what archaeologists do,
9920: somehow there is a position from outside theory from which arguments can
9921: be evaluated and more broadly archaeology can be practiced. I have
9922: insisted over and over again in this book that all archaeologists are
9923: theorists, whether we like it or not. There are many different possible
9924: positions to take – but they are all theoretical ones.
9925: 
9926: More profoundly, all these positions imply an assertion of community.
9927: What I mean by this is that they assert theoretical criteria that ‘we
9928: all know’. We all know what we read in a site report; we all know a
9929: ‘plausible’ explanation when we see one. But who are ‘we’ in this
9930: analysis? When the great seventeenth-century empiricists Bacon, Hobbes
9931: and Locke used simple, direct, ‘plain English’ phrases like ‘we can see
9932: that …’ they were actually writing for and to a very narrow circle of
9933: their friends and contemporaries male members of the English gentry
9934: classes. The most important lesson of multiculturalism, of diversity
9935: issues, and of engagement with Indigenous perspectives, has been that
9936: who ‘we’ are cannot be taken for granted. Indeed, ‘we’ can be argued to
9937: be the most theory-laden word in the English language. It is certainly
9938: one of the most ambiguous.
9939: 
9940: If a naïve empiricism is so obviously flawed, why does it endure? I
9941: suggest that its endurance is partly due to the fact that deep down, at
9942: a discursive and even emotional level, we are all empiricists. Two of
9943: the distinctive features of archaeology are its nature as a field-based
9944: discipline, and its engagement with material objects. Put very crudely,
9945: ‘we’, archaeologists, love the physical activity of digging, love
9946: handling old objects, love the feel, touch and smell of the past.
9947: However much archaeologists tell themselves that objects in themselves
9948: tell us nothing about the past (as discussed in chapter 2), they still
9949: love working with things, and ‘deep down’, at a visceral level,
9950: archaeologists can hear the artefacts speaking to them, without the need
9951: for intervening theory.
9952: 
9953: Part of the enduring strength, then, of the empiricist tradition is that
9954: however easy it is to condemn intellectually, empiricism remains present
9955: in a large part of the archaeological practice of everybody.
9956: 
9957: Processual and Postprocessual Archaeologies
9958: 
9959: A second way of approaching the question of where we are now is to ask
9960: what has changed since the turn of the century. It is possible to assert
9961: that the so-called ‘processual/postprocessual wars’ that characterized
9962: much of theoretical debate in the English-speaking world have ended (see
9963: figures 13.1 and 13.2). In the opinion of many, including myself, the
9964: term ‘postprocessual archaeology’ ceased to have much meaning after
9965: about 2000. The term still crops up, usually in critical comments by
9966: self-proclaimed opponents of ‘postprocessualism’ that are to my mind
9967: rather out-of-date.
9968: 
9969: Figure 13.1 Archaeological theory in 1988
9970: 
9971: [c13_image001.jpg]
9972: 
9973: Figure 13.2 Archaeological theory in 1998
9974: 
9975: [c13_image002.jpg]
9976: 
9977: There has been a growing articulation of a shared terrain or a shared
9978: set of concerns in much of archaeological theory. In the late 1990s I
9979: was suspicious of this move, for two reasons. First, I was not convinced
9980: by some of the case studies that claimed to back up this asserted middle
9981: ground. It seemed to me to be a wishful assertion, a piece of rhetoric
9982: even, rather than a seriously argued and substantive position. Second, I
9983: was unduly influenced by the depressing experience of reading countless
9984: student essays, and quite a few academic papers, that substituted
9985: phrases like ‘there is something to be said for both sides’ for any kind
9986: of intelligent and informed evaluation of the arguments. Ten years
9987: later, I have modified my views in this respect.
9988: 
9989: We saw in chapter 5 how thinking in terms of cultural process had
9990: shifted, away from ecosystem models, and towards a much more nuanced
9991: understanding, taking on concerns about agency, conflict, and gender. At
9992: the same time, much of the early programmatic polemic of
9993: postprocessualism had faded away by 2000. Angry claims and
9994: counter-claims about relativism and its dangers had largely subsided.
9995: 
9996: One version of this articulation has been termed ‘processual plus’ by
9997: Michelle Hegmon in relation to North American archaeological theory.
9998: Hegmon argued that much of North American archaeology had taken on the
9999: themes and issues of gender, agency, symbolism, and materiality, and had
10000: engaged with the political and cultural context of archaeology. She drew
10001: attention to the way processual themes and language had shifted: away
10002: from the evolution of culture to diverse trajectories of change, from
10003: social organization to social strategies, from humans in the environment
10004: to humans as part of the environment. (I would add to her list a shift
10005: from a language of ‘testing’ to a language of ‘evaluation’.)
10006: 
10007: Hegmon’s views drew a quick response from others. While Hegmon insisted
10008: that ‘I am not suggesting that there is a theoretical rapprochement …
10009: nor do I think that this kind of homogeneity would be a good thing’
10010: (Hegmon 2003: 233), others felt that it left important and dissonant
10011: elements out, in particular those elements of theory that engage with
10012: social justice issues, including Indigenous, Marxist, feminist and
10013: postcolonial archaeologies; it could be added that Hegmon was also open
10014: about leaving behavioural and Darwinian archaeologies out of her
10015: synthesis.
10016: 
10017: I think that Hegmon’s assessment is important in two respects. First,
10018: she is looking for a way to move theoretical debate forward
10019: constructively. Conflicts are not necessarily bad; in many respects, a
10020: discipline can be defined not through its common ground but through its
10021: disagreements – for example the Obeyesekere/Sahlins debate in cultural
10022: anthropology, or the origins of the English Revolution or the
10023: feudal/capitalist transition in history. However, an inclusive and
10024: diverse discipline, and above all a mature discipline, should be able to
10025: move its theoretical debates beyond a clash of schools or what she calls
10026: ‘egos’ in which the winner takes all and the loser is crushed. Second,
10027: she is correct in her assertion that a series of common themes can be
10028: seen to emerge across theoretical battle-lines in the early twenty-first
10029: century. I will discuss three as examples: agency, materiality, and
10030: field practice.
10031: 
10032: Agency
10033: 
10034: The first common theme that can be said to have emerged is an interest
10035: in human agency and the individual human subject. This interest spans
10036: theoretical divides. Darwinists are interested in the individual
10037: organism, and in the role of chance and contingency in evolution.
10038: Co-evolutionary explanations examine the interactions of individual
10039: variation and cultural innovation, and the population as a whole.
10040: Faction, gender and other agencies have come to the fore in processual
10041: thought about the origin of social complexity and states. Giddens’
10042: structuration theory has been deployed in different theoretical
10043: traditions, while phenomenology rests at some level on an exploration of
10044: human subjectivity.
10045: 
10046: Agency is a classic example of theory being counterintuitive, of
10047: pointing out insights that run counter to orthodoxy, but which appear
10048: simple when thought about in a different way. One of the most frequent
10049: clichés heard is that we cannot ‘see’ the individual in the
10050: archaeological record. However, pots are made by people: in a very large
10051: part, the archaeological record contains very direct evidence of
10052: individual actions and human intentions the digging of a rubbish pit,
10053: the construction of house foundations, the manufacture of objects, the
10054: deposition of grave goods.
10055: 
10056: Stated in this way, an interest in agency sounds quite obvious and
10057: undeniable. However, over and over again in this book, we have seen
10058: different theoretical attitudes leading us away from agency. Basic
10059: archaeological classification into phases, assemblages and cultures
10060: leads us away from seeing the very simple human actions that lay behind
10061: the production, use and loss of, for example, Mazaokeyiwin’s awl handle,
10062: and towards their aggregation and assimilation into broader categories
10063: and processes.
10064: 
10065: Materiality
10066: 
10067: The second common theme is an engagement with materiality. Put very
10068: broadly and simplistically, a concern with materiality is a stress on
10069: the material world as important and in some sense active. Interest in
10070: materiality comes from a wide range of theoretical sources. Political
10071: economists are interested in property and expressions of value; Marxism
10072: is a materialist philosophy; phenomenological approaches stress the
10073: physical experience of the world and the intransigence of artefacts;
10074: behavioural archaeology examines the relationships between people and
10075: things in all times and places. All these approaches share common
10076: ground. They insist that the relationship between people and things is
10077: important, that it is complex, and that things (objects, buildings,
10078: monuments, landscapes) are not simply passive reflections of other
10079: aspects of culture.
10080: 
10081: Archaeology has struggled in the past to free itself from a series of
10082: approaches that saw the material world and material objects as somehow
10083: secondary or less important. In chapter 6 we saw how the idealist
10084: philosophy of Croce, in which ideas were more important than things,
10085: implied that archaeologists should content themselves with description
10086: and classification. In culture history, we saw how for the normative
10087: approach artefacts are (imperfect) reflections of ‘cultural norms’ which
10088: exists inside people’s heads. In many processual views, artefacts are
10089: the fossil record of past ‘processes’ which do not necessarily have a
10090: direct physical existence, and which again are defined either in
10091: ‘adaptive’ or in ‘social’ terms. Within an interpretive framework,
10092: artefacts are often held to ‘represent’, ‘reflect’ or ‘express’ social
10093: values or meanings, values or meanings which are implicitly held to have
10094: a prior existence.
10095: 
10096: All these views share common ground, in that they tend to view the
10097: archaeological record as somehow a by-product of what archaeologists are
10098: really interested in (human history, cultural process). Arguably, then,
10099: they all implicitly treat the subject-matter of archaeology, old things,
10100: as less important or somehow inferior or secondary to other disciplines.
10101: In a more popular sense, traditional archaeologists of Classical and
10102: historical periods often look in the material record for evidence of a
10103: process that is pre-defined through the documents – thus an ‘archaeology
10104: of lordship’ concerns itself with castles and village layout, an
10105: ‘archaeology of the Roman Empire’ with forts, villas and cities. These
10106: approaches appear to be an advance on traditional models, in that they
10107: are ‘social’ or ‘anthropological’ in nature, referring to social
10108: structures and processes rather than simply historical narratives. But
10109: they still treat the world as epiphenomenal, or in other words a
10110: secondary by-product of what is really important.
10111: 
10112: A concern with materiality proposes, first, that the material world is
10113: important, and second that it cannot be comprehended using previous
10114: models such as ‘text’ or ‘norms’. In this view, objects mediate social
10115: relationships, or ‘materialize’ them. It flows, in part, out of the
10116: concerns with phenomenology and subjective experience, and the role that
10117: material objects and landscape play in constituting and mediating that
10118: experience, discussed in chapter 7. A key preoccupation is with
10119: ontology, that is, the study of the being or essence of things, or of
10120: being in the abstract. Objects are seen as active, and as having agency.
10121: Objects are also seen as changing the human subject – a man with a gun
10122: is a very different being from an unarmed man.
10123: 
10124: One particularly strong tendency within the concern with materiality has
10125: been the recent emergence of a school of ‘symmetrical archaeology’.
10126: Symmetrical archaeology ‘begins with the proposition: humans and
10127: nonhumans should not be regarded as ontologically distinct, as detached
10128: and separated entities … neither our analyses nor our explanations nor
10129: our interpretations should ever begin with such asymmetric dualisms.
10130: Thought and action, ideas and materials, past and present are thoroughly
10131: mixed ontologically. … Any radical separation, opposition and
10132: contradiction between people and the material world within which they
10133: live is regarded as the outcome of a specifically modern way of
10134: distributing entities and segmenting the world’ (Whitmore 2006: 546).
10135: The ‘actor-network theory’ of Bruno Latour has been influential on this
10136: point. Latour proposes that social scientists have been too ready to
10137: ascribe the causes of surface phenomena to an underlying ‘reality’ they
10138: call ‘the social’. However, he points out that ‘the social’ doesn’t
10139: exist in these terms: you and I have never touched, felt or smelt ‘the
10140: social’. Chris Witmore lists six characteristics of a symmetrical
10141: archaeology: that archaeology begins with mixtures, not bifurcations;
10142: there is always a variety of human and non-human agencies; there is more
10143: to understanding than ‘meaning’; change arises from fluctuating
10144: relations between entities; the past is not exclusively past; humanity
10145: begins with things (Witmore 2006: 549).
10146: 
10147: Symmetrical archaeology represents, for better or worse, a fundamental
10148: challenge to the way most archaeologists operate and to the theoretical
10149: underpinnings of much of the discipline, just as one of its key
10150: inspirations, ‘actor-network theory’, challenges the underpinnings of
10151: the human sciences. For example, this book has insisted, over and over
10152: again, that archaeology is fundamentally about human beings (it is
10153: nothing if not anthropological), and about ‘why?’ questions (we can do
10154: more than just describe and classify); it posited a radical separation
10155: between present and past (figure 2.1). All these assumptions are
10156: questioned by this perspective. I am profoundly sceptical of the claim
10157: that symmetrical archaeology can achieve a fundamental reordering of the
10158: discipline, but it does represent a hardening and unifying of different
10159: strands of theory into a coherent manifesto.
10160: 
10161: Theorizing The Field
10162: 
10163: The third common thread that can be said to have emerged is a widening
10164: and deepening of the purview of theory to include reflexive approaches
10165: to the everyday activities of fieldwork and artefact analysis. Two
10166: distinctive features of archaeology are its nature as a field-based
10167: activity, and its materiality, as discussed above. Archaeology derives
10168: much of its emotional appeal from these features. One of the most
10169: exciting and encouraging developments in theory, then, has been the
10170: opening-up of field practice.
10171: 
10172: The classic example of reflexive fieldwork is Ian Hodder’s work at the
10173: Neolithic site at Çatalhöyük in Turkey. Hodder has attempted to develop
10174: a ‘reflexive excavation methodology’, in several senses. First, the way
10175: of working is claimed to be ‘always momentary, fluid and flexible’
10176: (Hodder 1997: 691); day-to-day working methods include the keeping of
10177: site diaries, discussion and questioning of appropriate strategies.
10178: Second, there is an attempt to be open and inclusive to different
10179: interests at the site, from Californian Goddess-worshippers to the
10180: different local groups and authorities. Third, the site archive has been
10181: developed through relational databases and webbased material in such a
10182: way as to make it open and accessible, and to make clear the subjective
10183: elements behind the objective record or data-set. Çatalhöyük is a
10184: ‘flagship’ site and a well-funded project of the highest profile; but
10185: theorizing ‘the field’ has taken place in a wide range of other
10186: contexts. Gavin Lucas, for example, has considered the development of
10187: the theory of stratigraphy, while my own work has looked at the methods
10188: of English landscape archaeology as a form of field practice inspired by
10189: Romanticism.
10190: 
10191: More broadly, there has been much theoretical reflection on concepts
10192: such as ‘heritage’, ‘landscape’, ‘diversity’ and related issues in
10193: recent years. This reflection has emerged as much from professional
10194: institutions such as English Heritage or the US National Parks Service
10195: as it has from the universities. It has been prompted in part by
10196: changing legislation. In Britain, for example, there is now a statutory
10197: requirement on public bodies such as heritage organizations, museums and
10198: universities to explicitly reflect on issues of diversity and inclusion
10199: and to develop specific policies and programmes of action to promote
10200: these values. We saw in the last chapter how in the USA, the NAGPRA
10201: legislation has compelled archaeological practice to engage with these
10202: issues.
10203: 
10204: Where Theory Is Going
10205: 
10206: I have not selected the three strands of agency, materiality and field
10207: practice at random. It can be argued that they represent three different
10208: elements of moving-on rather than synthesis: ‘what is needed is not a
10209: merging or a fusion of processual or post-processual archaeology, but a
10210: moving on from a debate caught in a tired opposition between subject and
10211: object’ (Hodder 2006: 23).
10212:
