10213: My response to Hodder is very simple: he is right, but moving on from
10214: tired oppositions is a bit like trying to change the weather: easy to
10215: state as a goal, but a lot more difficult to do in practice. If I am
10216: correct and empiricism is enduring because, deep down, there is part of
10217: us all that remains empiricist (figure 13.3), then it makes such a
10218: moving-on all the more difficult. How might we undertake this highly
10219: desirable, but very difficult and laborious, theoretical task in the
10220: future?
10221: 
10222: Figure 13.3 Archaeological theory in 2008: the struggle between
10223: different elements of thought and activity, in the mind of every
10224: archaeologist
10225: 
10226: [c13_image003.jpg]
10227: 
10228: In the contemporary world, theorists have not had a good track record of
10229: influencing the future. Twentieth-century theorists had a very mixed
10230: record at making prescient observations about historical developments
10231: such as the rise of fascism and its attendant horrors, the continuing
10232: resilience of capitalism, the development of new technologies. I am,
10233: therefore, very reluctant to make predictions of my own as to where
10234: theory is going. All too often, such predictions sounds like
10235: wish-fulfilment – one reads over and over again of the imminent death of
10236: evolutionary approaches, of cultural materialism, of postmodernism, or
10237: even of theory itself. These predictions invariably come from opponents
10238: of the theoretical movement in question who clearly desperately wish
10239: that their baleful diagnosis is going to be an accurate one. But a few
10240: years later, the patient in question seems to be wandering around the
10241: landscape looking perkier than ever.
10242: 
10243: I would like to predict the continued implosion of avowedly
10244: ‘atheoretical’ approaches in archaeology. I would like to see a world in
10245: which no serious scholar can get away with simply dismissing ‘theory’ as
10246: irrelevant, or bypassing or trivializing the need to make his or her
10247: theoretical views explicit. Unfortunately, I don’t see such a happy
10248: situation emerging any time soon. In many areas, theory has been
10249: ‘accepted’ and there is a lively theoretical scene, but in others,
10250: theory remains an either/or alternative or an optional extra. Far too
10251: frequently, one reads comments like ‘X’s approach is very theoretical;
10252: an alternative is the more empirical approach of Y’. If theory is the
10253: order we put facts in, theory is not an either/or option. I am tempted
10254: to make the rather harsh comment that as a discipline, archaeologists
10255: cannot and should not tolerate colleagues who do not choose to engage
10256: with theory in some sense, any more or less than they could tolerate
10257: colleagues who denied the theory of stratigraphy or of evolution.
10258: 
10259: There is a simple step that advocates of theory could take to advance
10260: this situation: instead of sitting with gritted teeth through yet
10261: another exposition of Hallstatt typologies, we could ask two simple,
10262: courteous questions in every seminar we go to, without exception: What
10263: is your preferred theoretical framework, and what are your criteria for
10264: selecting it over possible alternatives? Why do you choose to put the
10265: facts in that particular order?
10266: 
10267: I am not being entirely serious here. There are good reasons why most
10268: academic papers do not begin with a general theoretical excursus, among
10269: them that they work within an accepted discourse or paradigm. This
10270: discourse or paradigm is accepted by the audience they are addressing
10271: and the networks they are familiar with. There is also an important
10272: argument to be made that theoretical traditions in some areas of the
10273: world are vibrant and important, but do not identify themselves as
10274: distinct, explicitly defined bodies of specifically archaeological
10275: knowledge or practice with a corpus of literature of their own. It can
10276: be argued that much of central and Eastern European archaeology, for
10277: example, does work within a sophisticated theoretical framework, but
10278: that explicit reflection upon that framework in such a manner as to
10279: enable the identification of a specific body of literature on
10280: ‘archaeological theory’ is problematic.
10281: 
10282: Progress and Impact
10283: 
10284: Does archaeological theory progress? ‘Progress’ is a loaded word, as the
10285: chapter on cultural evolution discussed, and perhaps carries too much
10286: cultural baggage and is best avoided. I do think that, as a discipline,
10287: archaeology has a record of developing better, more accurate, more
10288: robust, deeper, understandings of the past. And I think that
10289: archaeological theory has played a critical role in this process.
10290: 
10291: It may help to turn the question upside down. Can we claim that theory
10292: does not progress? I don’t think we can. Archaeological theory does not
10293: just ramble around in an endless and inconclusive ‘debate’ in which all
10294: views are possible and equal. Looked at historically, theory has moved
10295: in particular directions at particular times. This movement has been
10296: complex, and the moves made were very difficult to predict beforehand,
10297: but particular patterns, trends or processes can be discerned. These
10298: patterns or trends have been responses to major cultural and political
10299: questions of the time, but they are also in part responses to what
10300: archaeologists perceive as major empirical issues of the time, and they
10301: are also both constrained and enabled by the data.
10302: 
10303: One demonstrated way that theory has ‘progressed’ is in a broadening and
10304: deepening of its concerns. I am particularly interested in the
10305: vernacular practice of archaeological theory, as opposed to complex
10306: ontological questions of the essence of things. What I mean by this is
10307: that archaeology could do with more reflection on the everyday
10308: judgements and assumptions that are made by practicing archaeologists,
10309: and the way these reflect underlying theoretical categories.
10310: 
10311: Many of the conceptual categories that archaeologists use remain quite
10312: ill-defined. Indeed, the more basic and foundational they are, the more
10313: implicit and ill-defined they seem to be. This observation will not
10314: surprise cultural anthropologists, who are used to the idea that the
10315: more basic a cultural idea is, the less explicitly it is articulated;
10316: and archaeologists are nothing if not a rather peculiar culture or
10317: tribe. A classic example is the definition of ‘process’, first
10318: introduced in chapter 2, and returned to in chapter 5, which involves a
10319: cluster of related meanings. It is difficult to think of a more
10320: important term, at least in North American archaeology, and yet it is
10321: very rare to find an explicit and tight definition of it. Similar
10322: comments could be made about words like ‘type’, ‘phase’, ‘structure’,
10323: ‘change’. Ironically, the less overtly contentious an idea is, the less
10324: sharply it is defined.
10325: 
10326: Does archaeological theory have an impact on the ‘real world’, however
10327: defined? Of course, but the way in which it has an impact needs careful
10328: reflection. Part of the problem with assessing the impact of theory is
10329: its very complex relationship to practice on the one hand, and to the
10330: context of what we do on the other.
10331: 
10332: For example, one can ask: what active role did ‘theory’ play in the
10333: changes in North American archaeology seen in the last decade as a
10334: result of NAGPRA, changes discussed in the last chapter? One could argue
10335: that NAGPRA played a far more important role than the postprocessual
10336: critique in opening North American archaeology up to Indigenous and
10337: alternative knowledges. On the other hand, one could argue that ‘the
10338: postmodern condition’ was one part of the cultural and political climate
10339: that led to the passing of NAGPRA under a Republican President, George
10340: H.W. Bush.
10341: 
10342: One could deploy the deeper and more subtle argument that ‘theory’ is
10343: not necessarily a guide or a recipe-book, but rather an attempt at
10344: description rather than prescription. In other words, theory is an
10345: attempt to classify and comprehend the difficulties and contradictions
10346: of working within a particular context. In the case of NAGPRA, the
10347: legislation concentrated archaeologists’ minds – it made the
10348: intellectual necessity of engaging with Indigenous knowledge a priority.
10349: NAGPRA, the cultural and political context, ‘theory’ and archaeological
10350: practice moved together. Further, it did so in such a way that makes it
10351: difficult to specify cause and effect, but also in such a way as to
10352: demonstrate their close relationship – and to demonstrate the relevance
10353: and impact of theory, archaeological practice, and cultural and
10354: political context upon each other.
10355: 
10356: Diversity and Pluralism
10357: 
10358: In the first edition of this book, I stressed the importance of a
10359: diversity and multiplicity of viewpoints, and the importance of students
10360: thinking for themselves rather than accepting the approaches or
10361: positions that professors and other senior figures would like to hand
10362: down to them. I still believe in these points just as strongly. There is
10363: a necessary complement to be made. Theoretical views have to be argued
10364: through with reference to the literature, just as rigorously and with
10365: just as much careful thought as with any other academic endeavour.
10366: Theory should be democratic, but I do not believe it should be anarchic.
10367: It is not a place where one is free to pick whatever version one cares
10368: to choose – there is always a price to be paid, in terms of the time and
10369: effort required to gain deep knowledge and understanding.
10370: 
10371: Empiricism, the reader will recall, is (among other things) a division
10372: between the world of data and of words or concepts, and a rhetoric of
10373: prioritizing the former. In some ways, one of the most pernicious,
10374: pervasive and hidden aspects of empiricism in archaeology is the
10375: underlying proposition that while archaeologists have to work hard with
10376: the data – read the site reports, tramp across the landscape, handle the
10377: pottery and artefactual material, ‘master’ the stuff – we do not have to
10378: make a corresponding effort with our theory. We need to know our
10379: typologies and our chronologies, but archaeologists are allowed to get
10380: away with position statements and theoretical judgements that are not
10381: informed by the literature.
10382: 
10383: In the first edition I wrote that ‘Papers that end with the depressingly
10384: banal conclusion that ‘there is something to be said for both sides’ or
10385: that ‘we should look for a middle ground’ should be banned – not because
10386: a middle ground is in itself a bad thing, but because the search for
10387: such a middle ground all too often becomes an easy replacement for the
10388: hard work of serious yet sympathetic critique of one’s own and others’
10389: theoretical positions’. This passage was selectively quoted by at least
10390: one author to imply that I was advocating a rejection of any kind of
10391: consensus or middle ground; I was not. What I should have added, as a
10392: necessary and equal complement, is that the other phrase that should be
10393: banned from papers is ‘I just think that …’.
10394: 
10395: If one believes that theory is a part of archaeology that is as
10396: difficult and subtle as any other, archaeologists need to do the work,
10397: be familiar with the literature, and argue from a well-read and informed
10398: perspective. Archaeologists slip too easily from statements like ‘at the
10399: end of the day, it’s the facts that count’ to the position that
10400: archaeologists can make a judgment about theory that is not informed by
10401: the literature. In short, as Conkey and Gero stated for feminist
10402: research:
10403: 
10404: If we want to explore a configuring of contemporary archaeology, it is
10405: simply ‘poor research’ to ignore a large and diverse body of
10406: theoretical, analytical and conceptual possibilities that pertain to and
10407: substantively inform the questions at hand … ‘Do I have to do the
10408: readings?’ We would say the answer is, ‘yes’. (Conkey and Gero 1997:
10409: 426)
10410: 
10411: Archaeologists, then, can assert any theoretical position they like – as
10412: long as they are prepared for an immediate and rigorous interrogation
10413: into the basis of that position and the degree to which it has been
10414: informed by a detailed knowledge of the material. Theory is hard work,
10415: and it should also, if it is done properly, be uncomfortable work. Good
10416: theory should cause archaeologists to reflect on what they do, both as
10417: scholars and human beings. This reflection should not leave basic
10418: assumptions unarticulated or undisturbed: a response a theorist should
10419: aim for is: ‘You know, that’s really helpful, I never thought about it
10420: like that …’ In this sense, good theory should cause people to feel
10421: challenged about their everyday working practices as archaeologists, but
10422: also enabled and to think and act in new ways.
10423: 
10424: Archaeology is a broad church. A diversity of viewpoints is to be
10425: encouraged. On the other hand, there comes a point at which ‘diversity’
10426: becomes ‘fragmentation’. An excessive pluralism can appear to be
10427: tolerant, but in fact act as a cloak for suppression of debates – why
10428: should two views bother to engage critically with the other if both are
10429: ‘valid’? The most obvious example of a position that is simply
10430: unsustainable is the rejection of theory itself – I have repeatedly
10431: stressed how there is no choice to be made between ‘theoretical’ and
10432: ‘atheoretical’ archaeology.
10433: 
10434: There is also a balance to be struck between an insistence on doing the
10435: reading and a theoretical Calvinism. The sixteenth-century theologian
10436: Calvin argued for a form of Protestant belief in which only a very few
10437: people, the ‘elect’, could be saved. Theoretical Calvinists are as
10438: tiresome a breed as theoretical know-nothings. All archaeologists have
10439: to make choices about what and how much they read, just as they have to
10440: make choices about what to sample from the archaeological record. There
10441: is a regrettable attitude among a few theorists that if you have not
10442: read Lévi-Strauss in the original French, you have no right to comment
10443: on the broad thrust of theory. The literary critic Jonathan Culler
10444: writes that:
10445: 
10446: One of the most dismaying features of theory today is that it is endless
10447: … It is an unbounded corpus of writings which is always being augmented
10448: … Theory is thus a source of intimidation, a resource for constant
10449: upstagings: ‘What? You haven’t read Lacan! How can you talk about the
10450: lyric without addressing the specular constitution of the speaking
10451: subject?’… the completion of one task will bring not respite but further
10452: difficult assignments. (‘Spivak? Yes, but have you read Benita Parry’s
10453: critique of Spivak and her response?’). (Culler 1997: 15)
10454: 
10455: Given the necessary open-endedness and indeterminacy of theory, and the
10456: need that Culler identifies for the theorist to be constantly placing
10457: him or herself in a vulnerable position, aggressive denunciations which
10458: treat differing views not with respect but as ‘basic misunderstandings’
10459: are unhelpful. They can only be seen as a barely coded attempt at what
10460: Culler calls constant upstaging, leading to intellectual exclusion and a
10461: negation and closing-down of diversity.
10462: 
10463: It is important that these tensions between plurality and dogmatism, and
10464: over what and how one should read theory, be understood in pragmatic and
10465: pedagogical terms. In other words, the question is not just ‘what do we,
10466: as archaeologists, want to do?’ or ‘what is the essence of
10467: archaeological theory?’ but also ‘how should academic archaeology
10468: encourage students to be questioning – what practical strategies can we
10469: adopt to open up the discipline to dialogue?’
10470: 
10471: Conclusion
10472: 
10473: I came to archaeology as Roger Beefy, the bluff ‘dirt archaeologist’
10474: character who has been questioning things in this volume. Most of the
10475: practising archaeologists I had worked with before university were
10476: openly contemptuous of theory; in late 1970s Britain, ‘rescue
10477: archaeology’ was at its peak, and there seemed to be too much digging to
10478: do to worry about linear movement zones and mutual causal deviation
10479: amplifying processes. Of course, those familiar with the subsequent
10480: history of British rescue archaeology will be well aware of how mistaken
10481: this anti-theory view proved to be: how much of the material excavated
10482: and recorded was never adequately published and failed to contribute to
10483: better accounts of the past, in part because the lack of a broader
10484: theoretical framework within which to set research priorities and guide
10485: the writing-up and interpretation.
10486: 
10487: The first year of my undergraduate course, which was strongly oriented
10488: towards a narrow view of cultural evolution and the New Archaeology,
10489: tended to confirm suspicions that theory was just a waste of time. Grand
10490: evolutionary schema seemed boring and irrelevant to the particularities
10491: of archaeological practice. I’d read around history and historical
10492: sociology, and as Bruce Trigger also noted in his Time and Traditions,
10493: which I read around that time, the rejection of history seemed
10494: especially naïve. In retrospect, this was a biased and partial reading
10495: of the literature; but it was the one I made at the time.
10496: 
10497: This view was reinforced through experiences of theory outside
10498: mainstream archaeology. My work on the ‘English school’ of landscape
10499: history, which I first engaged with before university, and experience of
10500: historical theory drew me to an appreciation of how different
10501: disciplines really did have very different theoretical criteria and
10502: methodologies. In this sense, the insight that arguments are never
10503: evaluated in conditions of pure objectivity, but are always formulated
10504: and considered within a certain social milieu, was not a theoretical
10505: lesson that I learnt but a practical one derived from talking and
10506: working with archaeologists, historians, architectural historians and
10507: geographers. Again, practical experience rather than theoretical
10508: demonstration has led me to see a fragmentation of method within the
10509: discipline as a whole; I see very little in common between the method
10510: and theory of, say, the archaeology of the American Southwest and that
10511: of the medieval European church. Both would consider the methods of the
10512: other as utterly unproductive; if a ‘theorist’ has any role in
10513: archaeology, it must surely be to translate – to show how both schools
10514: have their own validity, their own intellectual credentials, and why
10515: both should respect the other’s traditions and take the time to talk to
10516: the other.
10517: 
10518: In the last decade, I have not changed my outlook on archaeology
10519: fundamentally, but I have shifted emphasis in my views in three areas.
10520: During this time, I published two books, one on the archaeology of
10521: castles, and one on the study of landscape. The critical reaction to
10522: these two books impressed two lessons upon me. The first was about
10523: epistemology: that rhetorical statements about the importance of the
10524: data, or conversely one-sided deconstructions of understandings of the
10525: past that exposed their political basis, were not enough. Getting one’s
10526: boots muddy and one’s trowel worn down told you nothing in and of itself
10527: about the past. The critical point was to develop better, more accurate
10528: understandings of the past. The way to do this (as any New Archaeologist
10529: would rightly have insisted 40 years ago) was not to follow one’s
10530: instincts, that is to simply excavate another village or survey another
10531: field and wait for the data thus gathered to speak for itself. It was to
10532: think clearly and critically about rigorously addressing how this body
10533: of data led to that explanation, and how the links between data and
10534: explanation might be strengthened.
10535: 
10536: The second was that the basis for any understanding of the past had to
10537: be, in part, through an informed, anthropological understanding of
10538: structure and process. The archaeology of castles and of the English
10539: landscape remain areas dominated by culture-historical approaches, and
10540: by methods that implicitly assume the primacy of documents as discussed
10541: in chapter 11. In attempting to articulate an intellectually coherent
10542: way forward for these fields of study, I found myself arguing in terms
10543: that stressed system, structure and process as a necessary backdrop to
10544: explorations of identity and practice.
10545: 
10546: The third lesson I have learnt is that the real theoretical struggle is
10547: not between different -isms, but rather, between a gut attachment to a
10548: naïve empiricism, and a cerebral insistence that we are all theorists.
10549: This struggle is all the sharper and more profound because it exists not
10550: simply between different advocates or schools, but rather within all of
10551: us – within the heads of all archaeologists (figure 13.3). There is no
10552: clear ‘enemy’ – there is, however, a constant and unceasing struggle
10553: between the comfort of what archaeologists take for granted and the
10554: ceaseless questioning of any responsible and mature discipline.
10555: 
10556: My main aim in writing this book was to persuade the reader of the
10557: excitement, importance and relevance of theory, and to encourage him or
10558: her to adopt a consciously critical attitude to the things she or he is
10559: taught by senior figures – ‘experts’ – about the theory and practice of
10560: the discipline. The rather naïve hope behind this was that a diversity
10561: of approaches to the archaeological record is a good thing, as the
10562: interaction of different approaches tends to produce interesting and
10563: fruitful results, a more truthful and more stimulating account of the
10564: past; and more broadly, that the intrinsic value of liberal academic
10565: study lies in its development of a positive critical attitude to the
10566: self and to society and culture as a whole. I don’t much care which
10567: ‘ism’ future generations of archaeologists find most appealing, except
10568: in so far as it enhances and fosters such critical attitudes. The moment
10569: an archaeological theory begins to sound like a recipe-book to be
10570: followed blindly is probably the moment at which it has outlived its
10571: usefulness.
10572: 
10573: What I do care about is debunking the enduring notion that somehow
10574: theory is an optional extra, or that it is an addition to or easily
10575: separable from the ‘core’ activities of archaeology. Theory is not the
10576: only important element of modern archaeology, but it is one of the
10577: essential ones. Or to put it another way, theory is a little like a
10578: visit to the dentist – to argue about whether it is enjoyable or not is
10579: to miss the point.
10580: 
10581: Though unlike a visit to the dentist, it can also be enjoyable.
10582: 
10583: Selective Glossary
10584: 
10585: Sir, I have found you an argument; but I am not obliged to find you an
10586: understanding. (Dr Johnson, to a tedious interrogator; Boswell, June
10587: 1784)
10588: 
10589: Many of the terms discussed below have led to confusion since they mean
10590: different things to different people. Terms like ‘class’, ‘culture’,
10591: ‘evolution’, ‘process’, ‘type’ and ‘power’ can appear quite simple but
10592: very often tend to mystify since they are used by the author in one
10593: sense and read in quite another. A good example is the term ‘theory’
10594: itself (see below). Part of the problem with such terms is that they
10595: vary their meaning according to context or disciplinary background. For
10596: example, ‘cultural materialism’ has entirely different, almost
10597: diametrically opposed meanings in the respective fields of cultural
10598: anthropology and literary theory.
10599: 
10600: I have only listed terms here that appear in the text but that deserve
10601: further amplification. I started out with the aim of compiling a full
10602: glossary of all the specialized terms I have seen used in archaeological
10603: theory, but it became rapidly apparent that such an exercise would
10604: require a further book. A number of terms (for example, materiality,
10605: perfomativity and reflexivity) are, I feel, better understood through
10606: reading the relevant chapter than through a glossary entry. Renfrew and
10607: Bahn’s Archaeology: The Key Concepts (2005) is extremely useful. Hodder.
10608: Shanks, Alexandri et al. (1995) has a good glossary. Further help with
10609: the human sciences generally may be sought in Johnson (1995), while Humm
10610: (1992) has a glossary on feminist thought. Lechte (1994) has brief
10611: discussions of contemporary thinkers. Raymond Williams’s Keywords (2nd
10612: edn, 1988) should be part of the reference library of every scholar –
10613: read his entries on ‘culture’, ‘nature’, ‘experience’ and your view of
10614: the world will never be the same again. The appendix in Culler (1997)
10615: offers concise views of the main schools in literary theory.
10616: 
10617: If many terms are not here, those that are have considerably
10618: over-simplified definitions. The best way to appreciate the full meaning
10619: and context of many of the words examined here is to read the relevant
10620: chapter(s) – terms can only be understood within the context of the
10621: intellectual movement(s) of which they form a part. Terms also change
10622: their meaning. Between my writing of these words and your reading of
10623: them, many of these terms will have subtly changed.
10624: 
10625: Agency The issue of how we think about intentional action and the
10626: resources needed to act, as in ‘structuralism lacks a theory of agency’
10627: (in other words, structuralism can’t explain why individuals act in a
10628: diversity of ways rather than blindly executing a preconceived pattern).
10629: Often paired with structure.
10630: 
10631: Annales school A French school of historical thought, part of which
10632: stresses the interrelationship of different timescales (the longue dureé
10633: or long-term, the medium-term, and short-term events of l’histoire
10634: evenementielle). Annales historians also focus on the study of
10635: mentalities. See chapter 10.
10636: 
10637: Antiquarianism In this book, mere collection of ancient artefacts
10638: without relating these to past processes. The antiquarianism of
10639: sixteenth- to eighteenth-century Europe and America was more
10640: sophisticated than this and demands more complex treatment, however.
10641: 
10642: Behaviourism The proposition that we should focus exclusively on
10643: observable behaviours and refrain from referring to people’s thoughts in
10644: our explanations of that behaviour.
10645: 
10646: Cognitive archaeology ‘The study of past ways of thought as inferred
10647: from the suriviving material remains’ (Renfrew 2005: 41). Cognitive
10648: archaeology claims to be more modest than the postprocessual search for
10649: ‘meaning’. It focuses on two areas: the evolutionary development of
10650: cognition in early hominds, and the subsequent cultural development of
10651: cognitive capacities, for example the development of writing.
10652: 
10653: Contradiction A term with Marxist connotations: see dialectic.
10654: 
10655: Critical theory See chapter 6. A school of thought derived in part from
10656: Marxism, associated with Adorno, Habermas and Marcuse, and often
10657: referred to as the ‘Frankfurt School’. Critical theorists aim to unmask
10658: the inner workings of a society which they suggest lie concealed from
10659: view by the mystifications of ideology.
10660: 
10661: Culture An archaeological culture is a repeatedly recurring assemblage
10662: of traits – pottery and house forms, burial practices – seen over a
10663: discrete time and space. It may or may not relate to a human culture. In
10664: the broader sense, a culture can be many things, including:
10665: 
10666: 1 a body of shared ideas and beliefs (the normative view);
10667: 
10668: 2 an ‘extra-somatic’ (outside the body) system adapted to the external
10669: environment (systemic view);
10670: 
10671: 3 a structure or code analogous to language; and
10672: 
10673: 4 an attribute of civilized behaviour, as in ‘every cultured person
10674: should have some knowledge of archaeology’.
10675: 
10676: Culture history A term usually used in North America to refer to a
10677: traditional approach to the mapping of cultures and cultural influence.
10678: 
10679: Cybernetics The science of energy flow, often associated with systems
10680: theory.
10681: 
10682: Decentring Humanists see individuals as autonomous, coherent and authors
10683: of their own meaning: the poststructuralist ‘decentring of the subject’
10684: deconstructs all these ideas.
10685: 
10686: Deconstruction A weapon from the armoury of poststructuralism. By
10687: showing that any word or sentence has many meanings and that those
10688: meanings themselves refer to a multiplicity of other meanings within
10689: language, any text, however apparently obvious or banal, can be
10690: ‘deconstructed’ or shown to have many meanings other than its apparent
10691: one. Decon struction can be used in politically radical ways (to show
10692: for example how the word ‘masculine’ is both unstable and privileged)
10693: though it has also been held to lead to a disabling and politically
10694: neutral relativism.
10695: 
10696: Diachronic Through time (as opposed to synchronic).
10697: 
10698: Dialectic A complex term from Hegelian and Marxist thought. It refers to
10699: the contradiction or conflict of ideas or of groups within a wider
10700: framework. That framework collapses from within, resulting in the rise
10701: of new social or intellectual forms that in turn develop new
10702: contradictions. Thus, for example, Marx’s account of feudalism is
10703: dialectical since it suggests that there were contradictions within the
10704: feudal system that brought the whole system crashing down, to be
10705: replaced by nascent capitalism and its own contradictions. A dialectical
10706: view of social change or of intellectual development can be contrasted
10707: with an evolutionary or progressive view.
10708: 
10709: Diffusion The spread of ideas between cultural groups.
10710: 
10711: Direct historical approach A North American term, referring to the
10712: project of delineating culture groups in prehistory by working from
10713: ‘known’ groups in ethnography and ethnohistory back into the
10714: protohistoric and prehistoric past.
10715: 
10716: Discourse A set of rules of how to write and think that is specific to
10717: particular disciplines or institutions: thus ‘all the apparatus of
10718: empiricist discourse are mobilized in this article?…’. A buzzword among
10719: archaeologists influenced by Foucault.
10720: 
10721: Diversity Taken to refer to the acceptance and respect of differences
10722: between people, for example of gender, ethnicity, sexual orientation or
10723: religion. Often paired with inclusion, with reference to changing the
10724: cultures of institutions.
10725: 
10726: Empiricism Popularly, the belief that the data will ‘speak for
10727: themselves’ without the need for intervening theories. Often used
10728: loosely as a term of abuse to indicate theoretical naiveté in general:
10729: ‘Oh well, you’ll call me an old empiricist I know, but I think we should
10730: just dig and not bother about theory’. In its more sophisticated form,
10731: as developed in seventeenth-century philosophy, empiricism rests on a
10732: conceptual division between ‘things’ or ‘the real world’ on the one
10733: hand, ‘words’ or ‘concepts’ on the other, and the prioritization of the
10734: former. Also used loosely, interchangeably and confusingly with
10735: positivism.
10736: 
10737: Epistemology How we know what we know, or the validity of knowledge
10738: claims. For example, the question ‘can we ever really know what people
10739: were thinking in the Formative Period?’ is an epistemological question.
10740: 
10741: Essentialism The belief that there are certain attitudes or emotions
10742: that are ‘natural’ or biologically endowed, either to humans in general
10743: or to a specific sex; much debated by feminists and others. Thus the
10744: statement ‘in prehistory, men must have been more warlike as they lacked
10745: the mothering instinct’ is an essentialist statement. Essentialist
10746: claims are often supported with reference to biological arguments (for
10747: example, that men’s and women’s brains are structured differently);
10748: essentialist statements therefore often have a basis in sociobiology,
10749: evolutionary psychology or related schools of thought. Alternatively,
10750: they are derived from humanism.
10751: 
10752: Ethnocentrism Two meanings: the belief that one’s own values and
10753: attitudes have held true in all times and places; and/or the belief that
10754: one’s own culture is morally superior to others, not simply different.
10755: 
10756: Evolution ‘Descent with modification’ (Mace, Holden and Shennan 2005:
10757: 2). A term historically derived from Darwin and Spencer. When used in
10758: archaeological writing, its meaning varies. It can involve some or all
10759: of:
10760: 
10761: 1 the application of Darwinian principles to humans in the past; and
10762: 
10763: 2 the idea that human societies can be ranked on a scale of complexity,
10764: and that through time there has been a move from simple to complex. This
10765: can be linked with ideas of progress in human life.
10766: 
10767: Falsificationism The belief that while we can never ‘prove’ anything,
10768: science advances by coming up with hypotheses that it is possible to
10769: disprove or falsify.
10770: 
10771: Feminist archaeology The belief that gender roles are partially or
10772: completely socially constructed rather than endowed by biology, and that
10773: women in particular have suffered from oppression in Western society.
10774: This has the implication that: (a) archaeologists need to examine gender
10775: roles and inequalities within the profession; (b) archaeologists need to
10776: be more critical about biases and assumptions made about ancient
10777: societies; and (c) archaeologists need to question the way academic
10778: knowledge has been constructed in, it is argued, a ‘phallocentric’ or
10779: male-dominated manner. Not all archaeologists interested in ‘an
10780: archaeology of gender’ call themselves feminists, and not all feminists
10781: would necessarily fully agree with point (c) above.
10782: 
10783: Formalism A school of economic theory that suggests we can extend
10784: Western ideas of economic rationality to non-Western societies.
10785: 
10786: Frankfurt School See critical theory.
10787: 
10788: Functionalism The belief that the different institutions and practices
10789: of a human group are interrelated in a manner analogous to that of a
10790: body, so that the form of one can be explained by its functional
10791: relationship to other elements.
10792: 
10793: Hermeneutics The study of how we give meanings to cultural products, or
10794: how we interpret human actions and their products (for example, written
10795: texts, social actions, works of art, or artefacts).
10796: 
10797: Heuristic To find out, to learn or to throw up fresh ideas, as in ‘we
10798: didn’t gain any grand theory from this simulation, we just did it for
10799: heuristic purposes – it threw up some new ideas and possible
10800: interpretations that we need to test out in the next research stage’.
10801: 
10802: Historicism Various meanings. Often used as a code-word for Marxism;
10803: alternatively, New Historicist literary theory is ‘a critical movement
10804: insisting on the prime importance of historical context to the
10805: interpretation of texts of all kinds’ (Hamilton 1996: 2).
10806: 
10807: Historiography Various meanings, including historical theory and the
10808: history of historical thought.
10809: 
10810: Humanism Broadly, the study of human culture; often associated with the
10811: belief that there is an essential ‘human condition’ regardless of
10812: historical circumstance.
10813: 
10814: Hypothesis A prediction about the relationship between variables, as in
10815: ‘My hypothesis is that marginal environments and risk are positively
10816: correlated.’
10817: 
10818: Idealism Theoretical usage differs from commonplace usage. An idealist
10819: believes that thoughts are prior to actions, in other words that the
10820: mental world is more important than the material. In Collingwood’s
10821: historical idealism, the central method of historical and archaeological
10822: interpretation is to rethink the thoughts of past peoples
10823: empathetically.
10824: 
10825: Ideology See chapter 6. A set of overt or implicit beliefs or views of
10826: the world. According to Marxists, ideology serves to legitimate or mask
10827: the ‘real’ state of social relations.
10828: 
10829: Indigenous Of a people inhabiting a region with which they have the
10830: earliest known historical connection, often with a history of
10831: dispossession by later immigrants; a term whose definition is much
10832: debated, and therefore often used with a capital I.
10833: 
10834: Inductivism The belief that archaeological research starts with
10835: observations from the data, building general statements from them (in
10836: contrast with the HDN model, in which data collection is
10837: hypothesis-driven).
10838: 
10839: Legitimation See ideology.
10840: 
10841: Logicism A school of archaeological theory associated with the Ecole
10842: Pratiques des Hautes Etudes in Paris. Logicists focus attention on the
10843: structure of publications and interpretations rather than the
10844: archaeological remains themselves (see Gardin 1980).
10845: 
10846: Logocentrism For poststructuralists, logocentrism is the illusion
10847: fostered by positivist science that there is a concrete world beyond
10848: ‘the text’, to which the text refers, and that meanings can therefore be
10849: fixed in some final ‘reality’. Poststructuralists argue that meanings
10850: are never fixed in this way and deny what they call ‘the logocentric
10851: fallacy’.
10852: 
10853: Materialism Various meanings. Literally, a materialist believes that the
10854: material, physical world should be given more significance than the
10855: ideal or mental world. Some Marxists use the terms ‘Marxism’ and
10856: ‘materialism’ interchangeably. It is also used as a code-word by some
10857: North American archaeologists to indicate Marxism without its political
10858: connotations. For literary theorists, cultural materialism is the idea
10859: that culture and history are linked dialectically; it is sometimes seen
10860: as ‘the British version of New Historicism’, though it tends to be more
10861: openly committed to left-wing politics. Finally, materialism is used to
10862: describe an acquisitive mind-set that many archaeologists feel is bound
10863: up with the origins of capitalism, as in ‘the increased comfort of homes
10864: in the sixteenth century reflects a new spirit of materialism’.
10865: 
10866: Materialschlacht a term referring to detailed presentation of material,
10867: on the (empiricist) principle of letting the facts speak for themselves;
10868: increasingly used in German archaeology in pejorative reference to
10869: largely descriptive dissertation theses which catalogue copious
10870: archaeological material with very little interpretation.
10871: 
10872: Methodological individualism The belief that societies are nothing more
10873: than collections of individuals, and that therefore we can only explain
10874: ‘social’ phenomomena through individual psychology.
10875: 
10876: Methodology The techniques and methods used to collect and interpret
10877: archaeological data. Some feel methodology is part of theory; others
10878: insist that it be kept separate.
10879: 
10880: Modernism Used loosely to refer to belief in Science, Truth and
10881: Progress.
10882: 
10883: Multiculturalism The celebration of diverse ethnic backgrounds.
10884: 
10885: Multilinear Of a model that suggests more than one possible course, for
10886: example of social evolution.
10887: 
10888: Nomothetic (also nomological) Generalizing, as in ‘The New Archaeology
10889: had nomothetic aims’.
10890: 
10891: Normative The assumption that artefacts are expressions of cultural
10892: ideas or norms.
10893: 
10894: Ontology The study of the being or essence of things, or of being in the
10895: abstract.
10896: 
10897: Paradigm A set of beliefs or assumptions that underpin the whole way of
10898: doing ‘normal science’. The term was invented by Kuhn, who suggested
10899: that science was characterized by long periods of stability punctuated
10900: by revolutionary ‘paradigm shifts’, for example between Newtonian and
10901: Einsteinian physics. There is much debate about whether Kuhn’s
10902: assessment is accurate, and if so whether the New Archaeology (or for
10903: that matter postprocessual archaeology) marked a true ‘paradigm shift’.
10904: 
10905: Particularize To explain or understand something in terms of its
10906: peculiar qualities.
10907: 
10908: Phenomenology The study of human experience and consciousness in
10909: everyday life.
10910: 
10911: Polythetic With many features or criteria.
10912: 
10913: Postcolonial Postcolonial theory deals with the effects of colonization
10914: on cultures and societies, from a critical standpoint, and the issues
10915: these raise for academic and cultural enquiry.
10916: 
10917: Postmodernism According to Lyotard, ‘incredulity towards
10918: metanarratives’.
10919: 
10920: Practice A term closely linked to agency, associated with Bourdieu
10921: rather than Giddens, referring to everyday actions and their
10922: relationship to structure.
10923: 
10924: Pragmatism A philosophy originally developed by Charles Peirce and
10925: others proposing that the meaning of an idea or a proposition lies in
10926: its observable practical consequences.
10927: 
10928: Processual A school of archaeological thought that stresses the idea of
10929: process, tends to generalize and adopts a broadly positivist approach.
10930: Processualism can be seen as a developed, more mature form of the New
10931: Archaeology.
10932: 
10933: Realism A philosophical position which accords to known or perceived
10934: things an existence or nature which is independent of whether anyone is
10935: thinking about or perceiving them. In archaeology, realism tends to be
10936: adopted as a position between the extremes of positivism and social
10937: constructivism.
10938: 
10939: Reductionism The belief that complex phenomena can be explained by
10940: reducing them to a set of often very simple variables: often linked to
10941: Darwinist, essentialist or sociobiological arguments. Often used as a
10942: term of abuse by its opponents. Thus, ‘To suggest that we can explain
10943: the presence of weapons in these graves simply by reference to Man’s
10944: aggressive instinct is both reductionist and essentialist’.
10945: 
10946: Reification Taking an idea or cluster of ideas and treating it as a
10947: definite thing. Thus, ‘the Neolithic’, ‘the Formative Period’ and
10948: ‘postprocessual archaeology’ are all reifications. Usually used
10949: pejoratively.
10950: 
10951: Relativism The belief that all possible accounts of the past are equally
10952: valid in their own terms, there being no neutral or objective way to
10953: judge between them. Also used in anthropology as the view that other
10954: cultures are simply different from our own, not ‘better’ or ‘worse’.
10955: 
10956: Semiotics The study of signs.
10957: 
10958: Social constructivism Also known as social constructionism. The
10959: proposition that scientific knowledge is not a neutral body of data
10960: independent of cultural practices and values, but is actually created
10961: within society. In this view scientific data and practices are wholly
10962: social constructs (strong constructivism) or partly (weak
10963: constructivism). In practice, social constructivists are interested in
10964: laboratory practices and publication of information as processes to be
10965: studied sociologically rather than as purely ‘scientific’ debates in the
10966: narrow sense of the term.
10967: 
10968: Sociobiology The idea that cultural practices are biologically rather
10969: than socially constructed, based on the drive to reproduce genes.
10970: 
10971: Structuralism The belief that culture is governed by rules analogous to
10972: those of language.
10973: 
10974: Structuration theory A term associated with Giddens to describe how
10975: social agents or individuals relate to social structures. Giddens
10976: suggests that actors are constrained by their social environments, but
10977: pursue active strategies; the clash of agency then produces, often as an
10978: unintended consequence, change in social structures.
10979: 
10980: Structure Enduring cultural or social relations. Sometimes used
10981: interchangeably with ‘system’ by functionalists, though for Marxists and
10982: structuralists the structure underlies the social system.
10983: 
10984: Substantivism A school of economic theory that holds that our ideas of
10985: economic ‘rationality’ are not applicable to other cultures.
10986: 
10987: Synchronic At one particular moment in time (as opposed to diachronic).
10988: 
10989: Systems Can be used in a variety of senses from the very general (‘the
10990: social system that was the Roman Empire came under stress in the third
10991: century ad’) to very particular. In its particular forms, systems theory
10992: is close to functionalist beliefs that cultures can be seen as bodies –
10993: themselves systems – composed of various subsystems. Change in one
10994: subsystem will thus react against other subsystems and either be
10995: ‘dampened down’ (negative feedback leading to homeostasis) or produce
10996: wider change (positive feedback leading to transformation such as
10997: systems collapse).
10998: 
10999: Teleology A narrative with a definite development towards a point; for
11000: example, a traditional Marxist account of history is teleological in
11001: that it suggests an inexorable progress towards communism.
11002: 
11003: Theorifeindlichkeit Used pejoratively in German archaeology to denote
11004: distance from or hostility to theory
11005: 
11006: Theory General definition at the start of chapter 12. It has also been
11007: used specifically to refer to a particular kind of structural-Marxism
11008: formulated by Althusser and others; thus, E.P. Thompson’s essay ‘The
11009: poverty of theory’ was actually an attack on Althusserian Marxism rather
11010: than on theory as such
11011: 
11012: Totalizing A scheme of thought is totalizing if it attempts to place a
11013: set of diverse experiences within one large scheme applicable to all
11014: times and places. For example, social evolutionary models can be argued
11015: to be totalizing in so far as they place all human societies within one
11016: stage or another of a grand evolutionary scheme. Usually used
11017: pejoratively by opponents of such thinking.
11018: 
11019: Unilinear Following one course, as in cultural evolution.
11020: 
11021: Zeitgeist A very complex term from German Romantic philosophy. It refers
11022: to an essential or defining spirit, whether of an historical period, a
11023: ‘people’, or other cultural group or formation.
11024: 
11025: Further Reading
11026: 
11027: How To Read This Book
11028: 
11029: As stated in the Preface, this book is an introduction to archaeological
11030: theory. In this sense, the most important element within its pages is
11031: the Further Reading section below.
11032: 
11033: The main changes a reader will find in this second edition are as
11034: follows:
11035: 
11036: -   A slight change in style and tone. Theory is invariably a very
11037:     personal thing, and as Culler (1997) points out in relation to
11038:     literary theory, it involves placing oneself deliberately in a
11039:     vulnerable position. There is a very fine line between critique and
11040:     personalized criticism, and I have tried to take care in this second
11041:     edition to err on the side of caution in this regard. I have also
11042:     steered away from the emotive and ambiguous term ‘we’, preferring
11043:     the more neutral ‘archaeologists’ whenever possible.
11044: -   Emendation and updating of the first four chapters. To understand
11045:     where ‘theory’ is today, I remain convinced that one still has to go
11046:     back to New Archaeology; ideas of empirical enquiry and cultural
11047:     process that emerged from this remain, I think rightly, foundational
11048:     to much of archaeology. I’ve tried however to make the account of
11049:     the development of these ideas more tied to the present – hence for
11050:     example the renaming of ‘Culture as a System’ as ‘Culture and
11051:     Process’. Because much of the account in the first edition was
11052:     written in the past tense, it was easy for the reader to assume that
11053:     the ideas contained therein were somehow passé or superseded by
11054:     later (postprocessual) trends. This was never my intention – ideas
11055:     of science, variability, pattern, process, remain central to much of
11056:     archaeology now and in my view have to be understood as a foundation
11057:     to other areas. Conversely, postprocessualism has, ten years on, to
11058:     be seen more historically, as a movement of the 1980s and 1990s,
11059:     rather than as a term that is particularly meaningful today.
11060: -   More extended discussion of ‘traditional’ or culture-historical
11061:     archaeology. Although often describing itself in terms that often
11062:     makes little or no explicit reference to a specific body of
11063:     archaeological ‘theory’, culture history remains an important
11064:     approach, particularly in Roman and later archaeology and in much of
11065:     continental Europe.
11066: -   Division of the ‘evolution’ chapter into two, one on cultural
11067:     evolution, one on approaches variously labeled as Darwinist or
11068:     selectionist. This reflects a growth in applications of different
11069:     forms of Darwinian theory to archaeology since the mid-late 1990s.
11070: -   More coverage of theory outside the English-speaking world. The
11071:     first edition was a victim of its own success. My intention had been
11072:     to write a book that chose to deal principally with traditions in
11073:     Britain and North America, and to be open and explicit about this
11074:     (see the repeated references to this on ps. xiii, 116, and 210). If
11075:     I had known how successful and influential the book was going to be,
11076:     and how it has taken on (despite my best intentions) the status of a
11077:     key text, I would not have made this decision. This second edition
11078:     includes much more reference to work from around the world,
11079:     particularly in the Further Reading section. I have chosen to retain
11080:     an overall structure that, some will find, continues to reflect an
11081:     Anglophone bias; this seemed a lesser evil than attempting to write
11082:     a separate chapter(s) on ‘non-Anglo theory’. Inevitably the book,
11083:     like any book, still bears the marks of the author’s biases,
11084:     preoccupations and language limitations.
11085: 
11086: I encourage readers to take a critical approach to this book as much as
11087: any other. See thoughtful and critical reviews of the first edition by
11088: Trigger (2000) and Holtorf (2000), and responses to my views by a
11089: variety of scholars in Archaeological Dialogues: Johnson (2007) and
11090: responses.
11091: 
11092: General Points
11093: 
11094: When reading any scholarly book in archaeology, theory included, it’s a
11095: good idea to take a look at its reviews in major journals. These often
11096: tell you more about the prejudices of the reviewers than they do about
11097: the book, but are a good way of isolating areas of contentious debate.
11098: Articles in Current Anthropology and Archaeological Dialogues are
11099: especially instructive in this regard, since the article is followed by
11100: responses and responses-to-responses. Norwegian Archaeological Review
11101: also includes useful responses in succeeding issues.
11102: 
11103: American Antiquity, Antiquity, European Journal of Archaeology, Journal
11104: of Anthropological Archaeology, Current Anthropology, and American
11105: Anthropologist all have good theory articles. The Journal of
11106: Archaeological Method and Theory, Journal of Social Archaeology,
11107: Norwegian Archaeological Review, and Journal of Material Culture are of
11108: consistent quality. With the spread of theory in the last 20 years,
11109: other, more ‘empirical’ journals have ever higher numbers of articles
11110: that are explicitly informed by theory. Reviews of the literature on a
11111: given theoretical topic in the Annual Review of Anthropology are often a
11112: good place to start.
11113: 
11114: You can complement reading with attendance at conferences. The main
11115: British theory ‘talking-shop’ is the Theoretical Archaeology Group
11116: conference (TAG), which is held every December at a different university
11117: each year (the ‘archive’ is at (www.antiquity.ac.uk/tag/index.xhtml).
11118: Many go to TAG simply to see old friends and drink some warm beer, but
11119: it is also a ‘barometer’ of what’s the latest trend in theory today, at
11120: least in Britain. The majority of papers are given by research students
11121: or younger staff and organization is supposed to be ‘bottom-up’. The fee
11122: is currently £50 or less, there is usually free floor-space for students
11123: and the unwaged, and everybody regardless of status pays their own way
11124: to come. Andrew Fleming and I explored the history of TAG and some of
11125: the tensions between its egalitarian ethic and the reality in Fleming
11126: and Johnson (1990).
11127: 
11128: There is also a Theoretical Roman Archaeology Conference held every
11129: second year in Britain, and a ‘Nordic TAG’ (Tusa and Kirkinen 1992;
11130: Anderson et al. 1997). The German ‘Theorie-Arbeitgemeinschaft’ (‘T-AG’:
11131: www.theorie-ag.de) has a newsletter, regular seminars and also organizes
11132: sessions in many of the larger German conferences (see Härke and Wolfram
11133: 1993). RATS (Radical Archaeologists Talk Shop) is ‘an informal group of
11134: left-leaning archaeologists’ (James Delle, personal communication)
11135: rather than a theory group as such. It brings together archaeologists
11136: from the northeastern USA and beyond and meets on an irregular basis
11137: somewhere in New England or New York.
11138: 
11139: Otherwise you have to pick and choose between sessions and papers at
11140: major conferences. In North America, the Society for American
11141: Archaeology and the American Anthropological Association (SAA and AAA)
11142: hold symposia with theoretical content; the European Association of
11143: Archaeology does also.
11144: 
11145: There is a proliferation of blogs, wikis, and email lists dealing with
11146: archaeological and social theory, though these change and develop so
11147: rapidly that it is difficult to be authoritative about them. Sites that
11148: are likely to be enduring include:
11149: http://traumwerk.stanford.edu/archaeolog/;
11150: http://proteus.brown.edu/joukowskyinstitute/home; and the arch-theory
11151: email list. Fora of the World Archaeological Congress
11152: http://www.worldarchaeologicalcongress.org/site/home.php are an
11153: important arena. www.area-archives.org links to discussions of the
11154: history of archaeology, with theoretical content. The Archaeology part
11155: of the UK History, Classics and Archaeology subject centre has useful
11156: materials for both learning and teaching in archaeological theory:
11157: http://www.heacademy.ac.uk/hca/home. www.theory.org.uk hosts some useful
11158: – and hilarious – material on social theory and cultural studies;
11159: http://plato.stanford.edu/contents.xhtml hosts a collection of articles
11160: on philosophical issues.
11161: 
11162: The reading list that follows is partial, and will be out of date
11163: between the time I write these words and you read them. It is largely
11164: confined to English-language publications, and there is a bias towards
11165: publications that will be readily accessible in a range of locations. I
11166: have first listed some introductory readings, and then given suggestions
11167: for further reading on a chapter-by-chapter basis. The aim of these
11168: references is not to provide a complete bibliography on a particular
11169: theme; rather, they indicate the background to the arguments laid out in
11170: the text, and contain suggestions for further reading which all students
11171: should take up.
11172: 
11173: Introductory Texts
11174: 
11175: Praetzellis (2000) is tremendous fun. I wish I had written it. The best
11176: place to start for a historical perspective is Trigger (2006). I do not
11177: agree with Trigger’s assessment of where we are now (see Hodder 2006 and
11178: Wylie 2006, and others in Williamson and Bisson 2006, for friendly but
11179: critical comments), but his writings are consistently thoughtful and
11180: serious, and he has a good sense of the balance between academic and
11181: wider influences in the development of archaeology.
11182: 
11183: Renfrew and Bahn (fifth edition 2008) and Thomas and Kelly (2006) are
11184: excellent general introductions to archaeology with the merit of
11185: recognizing that theoretical concerns are integral to what we do. See
11186: also Renfrew and Bahn (2005). Gamble (2008) is accessible and gives a
11187: good sense of archaeology as an intellectually demanding discipline.
11188: 
11189: Binford, L. R. (2002) In Pursuit of The Past. This is well written, easy
11190: to read and gives a powerful, convincing account of his view of
11191: archaeology. Note that it was actually written by John Cherry and Robin
11192: Torrence from transcripts from Binford’s lecture series in Britain and
11193: the Netherlands – Binford is a brilliant, charismatic lecturer.
11194: 
11195: Preucel, R. and Hodder, I. (eds) (1996) Contemporary Archaeology in
11196: Theory: A Reader. A collection of 26 articles on most key aspects of
11197: archaeological theory. Very good for browsing. Second edition edited by
11198: Preucel and Mrozowski forthcoming. See also Hodder (ed.) (2001),
11199: Archaeological Theory Today.
11200: 
11201: For the overall thrust of Americanist archaeological theory at present,
11202: with its tensions and debates delineated as much as common ground, read
11203: Hegmon (2003), the articles by Watkins (2003) and Moss (2005), and
11204: Hegmon’s response in turn. It’s worth comparing this exchange with
11205: Kristiansen’s view of theory in Europe and the responses (Kristiansen
11206: 2008).
11207: 
11208: Meskell, L. and Preucel, R. (eds) (2004) A Companion to Social
11209: Archaeology. Oxford, Blackwell.
11210: 
11211: Preface: The Contradictions of Theory
11212: 
11213: Popular suspicion of ‘intellectuals’ is of course a precise inversion of
11214: intellectual fear and suspicion of the masses: see Carey (1992).
11215: Easthope (1999) is a witty exploration of the anti-theory bias in
11216: Anglo-Saxon cultural life. Sommer (2000) makes the important point that
11217: the definition of ‘theory’ as a distinct domain is itself a product of a
11218: particular cultural tradition.
11219: 
11220: Chapter 1 Common Sense is Not Enough
11221: 
11222: One of the most succinct and devastating demolitions of ‘common sense’
11223: can be found in Kluckholn (1939). The discussion of ley lines is drawn
11224: from Williamson and Bellamy (1983). See also Stout (2008).
11225: 
11226: Because most feel such debates are irrelevant, few ‘atheoretical’
11227: writers have been moved to put defences of their position in print.
11228: Fieldwork as a masculine discourse: Joyce et al. 2002: 18–26), and Moser
11229: (2007). Conversely, Conkey (2007) has explored the way the practice of
11230: theory itself is gendered.
11231: 
11232: An account of the difficulties of theory in another discipline, with
11233: which I find myself in general agreement, is Culler (1997) chapter 1.
11234: See also Eagleton (2003), with the slightly misleading title After
11235: Theory – really a critique of the move from Marxist to postmodern forms
11236: of literary theory. Bentley (2006) is a provocative comment on jargon in
11237: archaeology (see comments in later issues of Antiquity).
11238: 
11239: Chapter 2 The ‘New Archaeology’
11240: 
11241: I explore the links between Romanticism, empiricism (in the sense that
11242: the data are held to link directly to the past without the need for
11243: intervening theory) and historic landscape archaeology in Johnson
11244: (2007). Of Peter Ackroyd’s novels, Hawksmoor and The House of Doctor Dee
11245: are cult reading among archaeologists. Sir Thomas Browne’s essay can be
11246: found in Robbins (1972) and is discussed by Schwyzer (2007). Indigenous
11247: views of past and present: Watkins (2000, 2003) and Atalay (2006).
11248: 
11249: Further reading on archaeology before the New Archaeology: the
11250: often-repeated phrase ‘long sleep’ comes from Renfrew (1982: 6). Lyman
11251: et al. (1997a, 1997b) are introductions to Americanist ‘culture history’
11252: and reprints of selected papers respectively. O’Brien et al. (2005)
11253: discusses the development of North American archaeology from 1960
11254: onwards. I have avoided going in to the historical development of New
11255: Archaeology in any depth; such a project would involve looking at the
11256: anthropological/North American influences of White, Steward, Seltzer,
11257: Willey and Phillips on the one hand, and economic and
11258: geographical/British influences of Childe, Higgs and Jarman, Grahame
11259: Clark and David Clarke on the other. My thanks to Bob Preucel for
11260: clarifying this point.
11261: 
11262: Childe’s view of culture is summarized in McNairn (1980: 46–74). It
11263: should be noted that Childe’s thought changed markedly through his life
11264: – see his 1958 Retrospect. Grahame Clark’s thought developed over time
11265: from a stress on the economic (for example, Clark 1952) to a celebration
11266: of a politically conservative humanism (for example, Clark 1982).
11267: 
11268: Meltzer (1979) and Patterson (1986) are good introductions to the New
11269: Archaeology. Leone (1972) is a reader that includes most of the key
11270: early papers and gets across the diversity and excitement of early New
11271: Archaeology well. Binford’s early papers reprinted in his An
11272: Archaeological Perspective (1972) are essential reading for anyone who
11273: wants to understand the underlying mood and origins of the New
11274: Archaeology. His introduction and connecting passages are also
11275: entertaining and lucid, in contrast to his more formal academic writing
11276: style; see also Schiffer (1995: 1–24), for a rather different account of
11277: that time.
11278: 
11279: Flannery’s edited volume The Early Mesoamerican Village (1976) is an
11280: edited volume of case studies that over 35 years later should be read by
11281: specialists in all areas and periods. The case studies show New
11282: Archaeology in action, warts and all; see also Flannery (2006). Lyman et
11283: al. (2005) examine archaeological publication in Science and Scientific
11284: American, showing that New Archaeology had little direct impact on
11285: publication.
11286: 
11287: Read some of the texts, particularly in anthropology, the New
11288: Archaeologists turned to. White’s The Science of Culture (1949) is
11289: clearly written, and prefigures the processual stress on science and
11290: systems. The chapter on Akenaton is a brilliant demolition of the idea
11291: that individuals’ actions are random, unpredictable or important in
11292: history. Much New Archaeology was anticipated by Walter Taylor in A
11293: Study of Archaeology (1948); his emphasis on a ‘conjunctive’ approach is
11294: a forerunner of functional emphases.
11295: 
11296: Clarke’s Analytical Archaeology (1978: the second edition revised by Bob
11297: Chapman) is a difficult but essential read. It is hard to overestimate
11298: the influence of this book. Clarke’s 1973 article also had wide impact
11299: and sets the theoretical basis of New Archaeology alongside parallel
11300: developments in method and technique.
11301: 
11302: Archaeology as anthropology: there is an important recent debate on the
11303: position of North American archaeology within what is called the
11304: ‘fourfield approach’ – a configuration of archaeology and linguistic,
11305: cultural and physical anthropology. See papers in Gillespie and Nicholls
11306: (2003) and Segal and Yanagisako (2005). Brumfiel (2003) makes the case
11307: for a renewed link with anthropology and also identitifies some of the
11308: tensions from an Americanist perspective; see also Borofsky (2002).
11309: Williamson and Bisson (2006: 8–12), comment on the differences between
11310: Canadian and US archaeology. For the history of the relationship of
11311: archaeology and anthropology, see Gosden (1999).
11312: 
11313: Megaliths: Renfrew (1973a) is a popular account. It’s worth comparing
11314: Renfrew with more recent interpretations of megaliths – see, for
11315: example, Bender et al. (2007), Thomas (1999a), Bradley (1993) and Tilley
11316: (2004).
11317: 
11318: Hawkes (1968) is a short and in my view misconceived but influential and
11319: oftenquoted critique of New Archaeology from the perspective of British
11320: traditional archaeology. Klejn (1977) gives a more balanced view of the
11321: state of play in the 1970s.
11322: 
11323: Chapter 3 Archaeology as a Science
11324: 
11325: Frankenstein: Mary Shelley’s novel has been interpreted as a feminist
11326: critique of the moral limits of (masculine) scientific rationality: see
11327: Gilbert and Gubar (1979: 213–47) and Mellor (1988). A major caveat has
11328: to be added to comments on the disciplinary success of science from
11329: recent policy-making: the ongoing struggle over creationism and the
11330: political ‘war on science’ in the United States: see Mooney (2005) and
11331: Coleman and Carlin (2004).
11332: 
11333: On theory in Classical archaeology, see Morris (2004) and Shanks (1996);
11334: also Alcock (2002). Humanist distrust of science is often based on the
11335: implicit premise that what makes ‘us’ distinctively human (culture, art,
11336: religion) is precisely that which is ignored by science; therefore,
11337: uncritical use of science will move archaeology away from ‘the proper
11338: study of mankind’, that is ‘man’. See C. Hawkes (1954) and J. Hawkes
11339: (1968).
11340: 
11341: Positivism in the human sciences: Roscoe (1995) is a useful commentary
11342: on parallel issues in cultural anthropology. Bell (1994) takes a
11343: ‘refutationist’ approach though he is still positivist in the broad
11344: sense used here.
11345: 
11346: Kuhn’s philosophy of science is explored in a classic but long and
11347: difficult text (Kuhn 1962). For an introduction to the issues and a
11348: clearly written exploration of Kuhn’s significance in the human sciences
11349: as a whole, see Fuller (1992). For Feyerabend, see (1988 and 1995). I
11350: could have led the reader away from positivism via the work of Hansen,
11351: Quine and Putnam also.
11352: 
11353: Alison Wylie’s writings on epistemology (most collected in 2002) are
11354: absolutely central. They make dense reading, but this is because they
11355: deal with difficult issues and refuse to descend into platitudes. Gibbon
11356: (1989) is a comprehensive archaeological critique of positivism and a
11357: lucid view of a ‘realist’ position. Courbin (1988) argues, in detail,
11358: that there is no example of ‘testing’ in archaeology that can be shown
11359: to work in practice.
11360: 
11361: The literature on the ‘science wars’ is voluminous; for opposite ends of
11362: the spectrum see Gross and Levitt (1994), Wolpert (1992), and Latour
11363: (1987). Delanty (1997) steers a middle course. A summary can be found in
11364: Parsons (2003); it is striking there has been much less discussion of
11365: this topic since around 2000. Pickering (1984) is a brilliant case study
11366: but difficult to follow if you don’t have a scientific background.
11367: Feminist critiques of science include feminist standpoint theory, for
11368: which see Harding (2004) and Wylie (2006).
11369: 
11370: Recent archaeological work bringing together interpretive theory and
11371: scientific practice: Jones (2001); Sofaer (2007); Boivin and Owoc
11372: (2004); Jones and Macgregor (2002); Wheatley et al. (eds) (2002).
11373: Pollard and Bray (2007) reviews this recent literature. Davies et al.
11374: (2005) combine scientific evidence for woodland clearance with a
11375: phenomenological approach.
11376: 
11377: Chapter 4 Middle-range Theory, Ethnoarchaeology and Material Culture
11378: Studies
11379: 
11380: The discussion on analogy here is necessarily brief. It owes much,
11381: first, to Orme’s (1981) work stressing the large contribution
11382: ethnographic analogy has played in all archaeology however ‘traditional’
11383: and, second, to Wylie’s (1985) discussion, which demonstrates that there
11384: is no clear alternative to analogical thinking in interpretation. The
11385: new journal Ethnoarchaeology (first issue 2009) promises to be
11386: important.
11387: 
11388: The best place to start with Binford is his In Pursuit of the Past
11389: (2002, with a new Afterword, first published in 1983) before moving on
11390: to his more difficult writings, particularly Bones (1981b) and
11391: Constructing Frames of Reference (2001). Binford’s first essay on
11392: analogy was1967; his Nunamiut work is discussed in 1980, 2002 and more
11393: extensively in 1978. For the Mousterian debate, see Binford (1983b),
11394: chapters 4 and 5.
11395: 
11396: Experimental archaeology: Ethnoarchaeological studies of crop processing
11397: include studies by Hillman and Jones in Zeist and Casparie (1984); also
11398: Hastorf (1988), Miksicek (1987). For taphonomy, see Huntley and
11399: Stallibrass (eds) (2000).
11400: 
11401: Behavioural archaeology: Skibo and Schiffer (2008) is a good place to
11402: start. Schiffer (1976, 1987, 1995), Skibo et al. (1995) and Gould and
11403: Schiffer (1981). For the Tucson Garbage Project, see Rathje et al.
11404: (1992) and references therein. Skibo and Schiffer (2008) set out a
11405: complete manifesto for behavioural archaeology.
11406: 
11407: Hodder’s early work is in Hodder (1982a), though a more readable start
11408: to ethnoarchaeology is Hodder (1982b). He criticizes Binford’s use of
11409: analogy in (1982b: 20–4). Moore (1987) is important not just in its
11410: implications for ethnoarchaeology but in its use of feminist and textual
11411: theory in the interpretation of space.
11412: 
11413: There has been much recent interest in interdisciplinary studies of
11414: modern material culture: start with Tilley et al. (eds) (2006) and
11415: Miller (ed.) (2005); see also Miller (1987, 1995). Good introductions
11416: are Woodward (2007) and Tilley et al. (eds) (2006). Mauss and Schlanger
11417: (2006), Faure-Rouesnel (2001) and Warnier (1999, 2001) are good places
11418: to start on the French anthropological tradition of material culture.
11419: Olivier (2000) discusses memory. There are a host of excellent case
11420: studies in the Journal of Material Culture; there is a useful review of
11421: that journal from an archaeological standpoint by van Dommelen (2000).
11422: See also further reading on materiality, below.
11423: 
11424: Chapter 5 Culture and Process
11425: 
11426: Metaphors of culture: Toynbee’s twelve-volume A Study of History was
11427: fashionable in his day for his delineation of organic rhythms of
11428: civilizations, though he is almost forgotten now. The view of societies
11429: as embodiments of Big Ideas goes back to the nineteenth-century
11430: philosopher Hegel and beyond (Hegel 1956). Furholt (2008) is an
11431: effective case study critiquing the equation of archaeological ‘culture’
11432: with cultural group; see also special issue of Archaeologia Polona 34
11433: (1996).
11434: 
11435: Theoretical discussion in Central and Eastern Europe: Härke (ed.) (2000)
11436: and Gramsch and Sommer (eds) are good places to start. There is a themed
11437: section on Central European Archaeology in Transition in Antiquity 67.
11438: Kobyliñski (2001); Biehl, Gramsch and Marciniak (2002), particularly
11439: article by Neustupný; Hensel, Tabaczyñski and Urbañck (1998); Kuna and
11440: Venclova (1995); Neustupný (1993). Klejn (1993c) is a comment on German
11441: archaeology. Hänsell and Harding (1998) is an English translation of a
11442: selection of Georg Kossack’s essays. Gramsch (2000) is an especially
11443: thoughtful article. Klejn (1993b) comments on Soviet archaeology.
11444: Gheorghiu (2003) is a short but powerful plea for post-communist eastern
11445: Europe to resist the dominance of Western theory. Poland: Lech (1996);
11446: Tabaczyñski (2006, 2007).
11447: 
11448: For the intellectual sources of culture as a system, see the New
11449: Archaeology reading cited above, particularly Flannery, Renfrew and
11450: Clarke. Johnson (2004) demonstrates the continuing importance of the
11451: processual tradition. Early systems thinking: Earle and Erickson (1977)
11452: is a good collection of systemic approaches to exchange. Compare with
11453: Earle and Erickson (1982). Hodges (1982) is a processual approach to
11454: early medieval Europe.
11455: 
11456: Collapse: Tainter (2006) and McAnaney and Yoffee (2009) are both
11457: responses to Jared Diamond’s influential Collapse (2005). Schwartz and
11458: Nichols (2006) look at regeneration after collapse. Yoffee and Cowgill
11459: (1988). For the Classic Maya collapse in particular, see Sabloff (1990)
11460: as an introduction; Neiman (1997) takes a self-styled Darwinian
11461: evolutionary perspective; his arguments convince though I am not
11462: entirely clear what is specifically Darwinian about his theory. For a
11463: review of Maya literature see Fash (1994). See also Wilks (1985) for an
11464: interesting perspective on how archaeologists’ changing explanations of
11465: the collapse reflect contemporary political and environmental concerns.
11466: For Chaco Canyon, Sebastian (1996) advances a ‘sociopolitically based’
11467: model in opposition to the environmental explanations. Nelson et al.
11468: (2006) make the point from the prespective of cultural ecology that
11469: ‘reorganization’ is a more common response than collapse.
11470: 
11471: Habermas’s critique is much broader than is indicated here, extending to
11472: the nature of ideology in late capitalist society (see Habermas 1970,
11473: 1975; Bernstein 1985) and the nature of postmodernity. Giddens (1984) is
11474: another useful critique.
11475: 
11476: Revised processual thinking: the key paper is Brumfiel (1992). Crumley
11477: (1979, 1987); Brumfiel and Fox (1994); Brumfiel and Earle (1987);
11478: Renfrew (1986); Ehrenreich et al. (1995). See also broader collections
11479: of papers that balance generalizing approaches with emphasis on
11480: historical uniqueness: Scarry (1996); Flannery and Marcus (1983).
11481: Political economy: Earle (1997, 2002), Brumfiel and Earle (1987). Muller
11482: (1997) is a good case study. Bayman (2002) combines contextual analysis
11483: of the meanings of artefacts within a wider model of a prestige goods
11484: economy.
11485: 
11486: World systems theory: the general inspiration is Wallerstein (1974).
11487: Applications to archaeology include Kohl (1987), Kristiansen (1998), and
11488: Kristiansen and Larsen (1987). Kristiansen and Larsson (2005) is
11489: particularly interesting – many of the reviews of this book are coded
11490: evaluations of whether an ‘archaeology of Europe’ at a WST scale is
11491: intellectually admissible. World systems theory in Mesoamerica:
11492: Schortman and Urban (1994).
11493: 
11494: Chaos and complexity theory: Kohler (1993); Reisch (1991); Gerding and
11495: Ingemark (1997).
11496: 
11497: On ‘freedom’ and slavery, see Patterson (1982). I am grateful to Bob
11498: Preucel for drawing my attention to this point.
11499: 
11500: Chapter 6 Thoughts and Ideologies
11501: 
11502: Hawkes (1954) for his seven ‘levels of inference’ and the difficulty of
11503: looking at thoughts. Binford’s views on the inadmissibility of cognition
11504: as a factor in archaeological explanation are best expressed in his
11505: paper in Man (1987) and his brief Commentary in Renfrew and Shennan
11506: (1982). Fogelin (2007) is a useful review of different approaches to
11507: ritual and religion.
11508: 
11509: Terrenato (ed.) (2000) brings out the importance of Croce and the
11510: idealist tradition (and the consequent reaction against idealism by
11511: materialist scholars) in Italian archaeology, particularly papers by
11512: Sestieri, Cuozzo, Guidi and Terrenato. There are useful papers also by
11513: Maischberger and Tabaczinsky on German and Polish archaeology
11514: respectively. See also Guidi 1996.
11515: 
11516: Structuralism: Bradbury (1987) is a brilliant parody. Conkey (1989) is a
11517: good summary of structuralist concerns, tied to the example of cave art.
11518: Other examples of structural analysis are discussed in other chapters,
11519: but examples include Glassie (1975), Deetz (1977), Washburn (1983).
