11520: Bapty and Yates (1990) explore the implications of poststructuralism.
11521: French archaeological theory has not been as influenced by French
11522: structural traditions as one might expect: Demoule (1999), Fleury-Ilett
11523: (1993), Coudart (1999); see also special issue of Antiquity vol. 73
11524: (1999). Logicism: J-C Gardin (1980, 2002).
11525: 
11526: Marxism: Giddens (1979) is still a clear summary of the key points of
11527: classical Marxism; it is best to get a grasp of classical Marxism, and
11528: better still to read Marx himself (McLellan (ed.) 1977), before moving
11529: on to more recent strands of thought and to archaeological applications.
11530: Bloch (1983) discusses parallel debates in anthropology. McGuire (1992)
11531: is a sophisticated discussion. Childe is the most famous Marxist
11532: archaeologist – he combined a materialist model close to traditional
11533: Marxism with a diffusionary element. See Childe (1958, 1979), his
11534: ‘popular’ books (1936, 1942) and discussions in McNairn (1980) and
11535: Trigger (1980). For Roman Italy and the West, see papers in Keay and
11536: Terrenato (eds) 2001.
11537: 
11538: Ideology: Eagleton (1991) reviews the concept in general terms in an
11539: accessible and entertaining way. The writings of Mark Leone and the work
11540: of the Archaeology in Annapolis Project are the best examinations of
11541: ideology in archaeology. Start with Leone (2005) and move on to 1984,
11542: 1988 and 1995, and Potter (1994). See also McGuire (1992, 2008), Miller
11543: and Tilley (1984) and references for chapter 11.
11544: 
11545: ‘Cognitive archaeology’: the best place to start is Renfrew (2007).
11546: Renfrew and Zubrow (1994) styles itself a collection of papers
11547: addressing cognition from a processual viewpoint, though in my view the
11548: intellectual affiliations of the various contributors are more diverse.
11549: Flannery and Marcus (1993, 1976) are important studies. Davidson and
11550: Noble (1996); Brown (1997) is a review of a variety of approaches as
11551: applied to the Eastern Woodlands of North America. Cognitive archaeology
11552: and human evolution: an important general book, written by a historian,
11553: is Smail (2008). Gamble (2007) is an important study that every educated
11554: person should read; see also Mithen (1997, 2005). Many of the key papers
11555: for both ‘cognitive’ and ‘postprocessual’ approaches are collated in
11556: Whitley (1998). De Beaune, Coolidge and Wynn (eds) (2009), and Renfrew,
11557: Frith and Malafouris (eds) (2009), are up-to-date collections of papers;
11558: see also Coimbra and Dimitriadis (eds) (2008) and Mellars and Gibson
11559: (1997).
11560: 
11561: Chapter 7 Postprocessual and Interpretive Archaeologies
11562: 
11563: The place to start is Hodder and Hutson (2003). Origins: the interview
11564: with Ian Hodder in Hodder, Karlsson and Olsen (2008) explains something
11565: of the origins of postprocessual archaeology. For problems of testing
11566: and simulation, see Hodder and Orton (1976); Hodder’s
11567: ethnoarchaeological work can be found in Hodder (1982b). Watson’s (1991)
11568: article pointed out to me the striking parallels between Binford and
11569: Hodder in their intellectual development.
11570: 
11571: Shanks and Tilley (1987 and 1992, first published 1988) had a huge
11572: impact on publication. They are both difficult, but absolutely central
11573: to any understanding of postprocessual archaeology. Social Theory has a
11574: summary at the back. It is worth asking as you read: most of this was
11575: hugely controversial when it was published: how much is controversial
11576: now? McGuire (1993) is not just an article about Marxism, but offers a
11577: useful survey of the relationship between different strands of
11578: postprocessual thought and their academic and social context.
11579: 
11580: Hodder et al. (1995) is an excellent overview. Many of the key early
11581: papers are reprinted in Whitley (1998). Hodder (1999) is good not just
11582: on how to do interpretive archaeology, but on theorized descriptions of
11583: what all archaeologists do in practice and how all those practices are
11584: underpinned by theory, ‘interpretation at the trowel’s edge’.
11585: 
11586: Historical idealism: Collingwood’s Idea of History (1946) is his
11587: definitive statement of historical idealism; his Autobiography (1939) is
11588: shorter, more readable, and makes the context of his thinking much
11589: clearer – and also offers a cogent example of the political dangers of
11590: relativism.
11591: 
11592: Agency: the current interest in agency is not confined to
11593: postprocessualism; just as cognition is attracting attention from
11594: processual thinking, so is ‘the individual’ however defined. The origins
11595: of concerns with agency are usually traced to Bourdieu (1977) and
11596: Giddens (1979, 1984; see Held and Thompson 1989 for useful critique and
11597: bibliography). I have always found the work of Erving Goffman (1959,
11598: 1971) far more useful and lucid and recommend it as an introduction to
11599: the issues surrounding structure and action. A different approach to
11600: agency and structure is taken from within the postprocessual tradition
11601: through the idea of practice (Barrett 1994).
11602: 
11603: Dobres and Robb (eds) (2000) offer case studies from a variety of
11604: theoretical perspectives, while a double issue of Journal of
11605: Archaeological Method and Theory 12:3 and 12:4 discusses methods for
11606: ‘doing’ agency; the papers by Pauketat, Tomášková and Owoc are
11607: particularly useful. Dornan (2002) offers conceptual clarification.
11608: Dobres (2000) is an important study of agency and technology. Object
11609: agency: Gosden (2005), drawing on Gell (1998); see also references to
11610: materiality. For criticism of the limitations of agency as a model, see
11611: Kristiansen (2004) and succeeding discussion.
11612: 
11613: Critics of postprocessualism: many early critiques were responses to
11614: Hodder and Shanks and Tilley rather than to postprocessualism as such.
11615: These included Earle and Preucel (1987); Kristiansen (1988); Patterson
11616: (1990); Wylie (1992a); Binford (1987); Knapp (1996); Preucel (1995); and
11617: papers in Yoffee and Sherratt (1993). R.A. Watson (1990, 1991)
11618: criticizes Shanks and Tilley from the angle of positivism. Klejn (1993a,
11619: b) gives a reactionary view; see the thoughtful response to Klejn by
11620: Murray (1995). The debate between Thomas and Mithen is revealing (Thomas
11621: 1988 and 1991; Mithen 1991). Englestad (1991) and Smith (1994) are
11622: critiques of postprocessualism from a feminist perspective.
11623: 
11624: For an idea of the variance between different postprocessual traditions,
11625: compare Bapty and Yates (1990) and Baker, Taylor and Thomas (1990), two
11626: contrasting volumes that arose from the same conference.
11627: 
11628: Case studies: Johnson (1996, 2002) are my attempts to apply a
11629: postprocessual perspective to the archaeology of late medieval and early
11630: modern England, areas still dominated by culture-historical rather than
11631: processual approaches. Tilley (1991, 1994) apply textual and
11632: phenomenological approaches respectively to data from prehistoric
11633: Europe. McGuire and Saitta (1996) approach the traditional question
11634: ‘were 14th century Southwestern pueblos egalitarian or hierarchical?’
11635: and show how an approach stressing ‘lived experience’ can move beyond
11636: such either/or thinking (though they would not give their approach a
11637: specifically postprocessual label). See also the succeeding debate in
11638: American Antiquity 63: 2. Duke and Wilson (1995) looks at North American
11639: Plains archaeology.
11640: 
11641: Phenomenology: Much of the phenomenological literature is quite dense.
11642: The best place to start is the essay ‘Building, dwelling, thinking’ in
11643: Ingold (2000), though Ingold’s thought is too diverse to be classified
11644: simply as a phenomenologist. Thomas (2004) and Tilley (2004) have been,
11645: rightly, profoundly influential; see Ingold (2005) on the latter, and
11646: also Tilley (2008) and Bender et al. (2007). See also Gosden (1994);
11647: Tilley (1994, 2008); Thomas (1999b). Ingold and Vergunst (eds) (2008) is
11648: also engaging and profound. Method: Hamilton and Whitehouse (2006).
11649: Criado Boado and Villock Vásquez (2000) explore the building of a method
11650: through an ‘archaeology of perception’. Snead (2008) is a case study of
11651: ancestral Pueblo landscapes.
11652: 
11653: There is also an extensive literature on the subjective experience and
11654: construction of place, in tune with but not necessarily formally derived
11655: from phenomenological concerns, for example Bradley (2000); Sturt
11656: (2006); papers in special issue of Journal of Archaeological Method and
11657: Theory, 11:1 and 11:2.
11658: 
11659: Criticism of phenomenology: Fleming (2006) is a generalized denigration;
11660: Brück (2005) and Barrett and Ko (2009) are more sophisticated
11661: evaluations. Ingold (2007) is a thoughtful discussion of issues of
11662: ‘materiality’; Whitley (2002) critiques the over-generalized use of
11663: ‘ancestors’; Insoll (2007) critiques the human/natural opposition. In
11664: Johnson (2006), I explore the intellectual and practical context of
11665: phenomenology.
11666: 
11667: Semiotics: Preucel (2006) is an exploration and archaeological
11668: application of the ideas of Peirce.
11669: 
11670: Chapter 8 Archaeology, Gender and Identity
11671: 
11672: There is a plethora of books on feminism in general. Humm (1992) is an
11673: anthology that gets across the variety of perspectives; Harding’s paper
11674: in that volume points to debates between different feminist philosophies
11675: of science. McNay (1992) is a powerful anti-essentialist account of
11676: feminist concerns. I’ve avoided going through the topic of sexist
11677: language; for the basic arguments see Spender (1980). Moore (1988)
11678: explores the background issues in anthropology with clarity and is a
11679: useful synthesis of anthropological thought.
11680: 
11681: Studies specific to archaeology: the most up-to-date assessment is
11682: Conkey (2007), which also lays down important challenges for the
11683: practice of theory in general; it could be read in conjunction with
11684: Nelson (ed.) (2007). Gero and Conkey (1991) is a definitive collection
11685: of papers with a lucid introduction to the issues. Among the best papers
11686: are Hastorf, Gero, Wylie and Handsman. Conkey and Gero (1997) is a
11687: useful review of the literature. Hays-Gilpin and Whitley (1998) is an
11688: excellent collection of significant reprinted articles. Wright (1996),
11689: Claassen (1992), Walde and Willows (1991), duCros and Smith (1993) and
11690: Balme and Beck (1995) are other edited collections. Franklin (2001) on a
11691: Black feminist-inspired archaeology. Karlisch (ed.) (1997) and Merterns
11692: (1993) on feminist approaches in German archaeology.
11693: 
11694: Issues of gendered archaeological practice: Gero (1985, 1991); Wylie
11695: (1993b); Hutson (2002) on citation practices; Moser (1996). Tomášková
11696: (2007) argues for greater feminist reflection on and transformation of
11697: field practice, with specific reference to mapping. History of women in
11698: archaeology: Diaz-Andreu and Sørensen (1998); Joyce (1994), Claassen
11699: (1994). Career issues: various articles in Reyman (1992) and du Cros and
11700: Smith (1995); Nelson et al. (1994); Wylie (1993); Gero (1985, 1988). Two
11701: websites provide a focus for British and North American women
11702: archaeologists: http://britishwomenarchaeologists.org.uk/index.xhtml and
11703: http://www.saa.org/ForMembers/InterestGroups/tabid/129/Default.aspx
11704: 
11705: Archaeology of ‘women’: Ehrenberg (1989) weds a traditional
11706: archaeological format to a search for ‘women’. A famous study is
11707: Gimbutas (1989, 1991); most feminists would distance themselves from
11708: Gimbutas’s arguments; see Russell (1993) for a discussion of the
11709: underlying issues.
11710: 
11711: ‘Processual’ approaches to gender: Brumbach and Jarvenpa (1997) use ‘a
11712: gender-resource mapping approach’ in which gender is seen as a variable
11713: within a more general ‘subsistence settlement model’. Again,
11714: Mobley-Tanaka (1997) develops a gendered view of pueblo architecture
11715: within a framework of ‘social stress’. Evolutionary approaches: Hager
11716: (1997); McClure (2007) combines cultural inheritance theory with a
11717: gendered analysis. Pyburn (2003) looks at gender issues in the ‘rise of
11718: civilization’.
11719: 
11720: Case studies (many of these could be put under the ‘postprocessual’
11721: subheading): Gilchrist (1993); Hodge McCoid and McDermott (1996). Gender
11722: in historical archaeology: Wall (1994); Gilchrist (1993); Spector
11723: (1993); Yentsch (1991). Seifert (1991) is an excellent edited
11724: collection. Tomášková (2006) and Robin (2006) are important case studies
11725: that show how gender permeates other historical categories of analysis.
11726: 
11727: Sørensen (2000) argues for a clear distinction between ‘gender
11728: archaeology’ and feminist concerns. Anthony (1995) criticizes
11729: ‘eco-feminist’ approaches though most feminists would disavow these
11730: approaches in any case. Nicholas (1994) makes pertinent comments on the
11731: Goddess myth. Gero and Conkey (1997), writing from a feminist
11732: standpoint, make some critical comments, implying that much gender
11733: archaeology has failed to take the feminist critique sufficiently
11734: seriously. Greater ‘feminist theorizing and engagement’ is also urged by
11735: Englestad (2007), a theme that is also taken up by a special issue of
11736: the Journal of Archaeologcial Method and Theory 14.
11737: 
11738: Archaeology and identity: Insoll (ed.) (2007); Casella and Fowler
11739: (2005); Immonen (2005) for the case study of the Hanse. Hinton (2005),
11740: though it does not wear its theory on its sleeve, is a nuanced study of
11741: material culture and identity in the Middle Ages. Revell (2009) explores
11742: ‘Roman’ cultural identities. A special double issue of the Journal of
11743: Material Culture examines isues of identity as they relate to space and
11744: place: 11:1 and 11:2.
11745: 
11746: Masculinity: Johnson (2002); Wilkie (2010). Nottveit (2006) is a good
11747: case study on medieval daggers and masculine identity. Childhood: Sofaer
11748: (ed.) (2000); see also the new Journal of Childhood in the Past. Race:
11749: Orser (2004); Franklin (2001). Sexuality: Meskell (1999) argues that
11750: even ‘sex’ is culturally constructed. Voss (2008a) is a useful review of
11751: archaeological studies of sexuality, while Meskell (2002) is an
11752: important case study. Queer archaeologies: Dowson (ed.) (2000).
11753: Theoretical approaches to the body: Sofaer (2006). Crossland (2009) is a
11754: fascinating case study. For an archaeology of disability, see Hubert
11755: (ed.) (2000). An important critique of the social model of disability,
11756: and a readable introduction to disability studies, is Shakespeare
11757: (2006).
11758: 
11759: Performativity: Judith Butler is the key thinker, though her writing
11760: style is not easy. Butler (1990) and (1993) are classics that underpin
11761: much of the recent work on identity.
11762: 
11763: Chapter 9 Archaeology and Cultural Evolution
11764: 
11765: Pluciennik (2005) is an excellent, highly critical general introduction
11766: to social evolution. Chapman 2003 is the most authoritative recent book
11767: on social complexity. Yoffee (2005) is a robust ‘apology’ for cultural
11768: evolution, building in diversity to state studies. Peregrine (2001) is a
11769: stout defence of a unilinear and cross-cultural approach to social
11770: evolution. Trigger (1997) reviews the history of social evolutionary
11771: ideas. For a sample of current ‘state of the art’ approaches see Kohring
11772: and Wynne-Jones (eds) (2007).
11773: 
11774: Recent reviews of the literature include Stanish (2001) for state
11775: societies in south America and Cobb (2003) for Mississsippian chiefdoms.
11776: Smith (2004) discusses ancient state economies.
11777: 
11778: Earle (2000): cross-cultural survey of analyses of property within a
11779: politicaleconomic framework. Beck et al. (2007) is a good example of a
11780: combination of structural and agent-centred analysis. Rousseau (2006)
11781: also combines human agency and evolutionary theory. Again, Ensor (2000)
11782: stresses the importance of daily life or modo de vida. Segmentation and
11783: war: Kelly (2000b), Flannery and Marcus (2003). Colonies: Hurst and Owen
11784: (2005).
11785: 
11786: Evolution of social complexity: a good overview of Americanist work is
11787: provided by Baines and Yoffee (2000), Crumley (1995), Wason (1994), Fash
11788: (1994), Kowalewski (1990), Kolb (1994) and Spencer (1997). Smith (2003),
11789: one of my favourite books, is a model study of how a different range of
11790: concerns from political economy can be integrated within a generalizing
11791: understanding of the ‘political landscapes’ of state societies.
11792: Criticisms of social evolution and a refined approach attempting to
11793: define and measure ‘inequality’ and ‘heterogeneity’ in societies is
11794: outlined by McGuire (1983). The studies in Part IV of Preucel and Hodder
11795: (1996) give a good flavour of the diversity of approaches to
11796: socipolitical complexity.
11797: 
11798: Latin American social archaeology: Lumbreras (2005) and Bate (1998).
11799: English discussions can be found in Patterson (1994); see also the
11800: response to Patterson (Oyuela-Caycedo et al. 1997) and Patterson’s
11801: response in turn, and McGuire (1993). Drawing on its terminology, Ensor
11802: (2000) stresses the importance of modo de vida. See also Oyuela-Caycedo
11803: (1994).
11804: 
11805: Cahokia: Pauketat (2001) uses the Mississippian as a case study in
11806: proposing a ‘new paradigm’ of ‘historical processualism’. Holt (2009) on
11807: Cahokia as a ‘theatre state’; also Brown (2006) and comments in Beck et
11808: al. (2007).
11809: 
11810: Criticisms of cultural evolution in archaeology: Yoffee (1979); Trigger
11811: (1984, 1997); Shanks and Tilley (1987); Patterson (1995); McGuire
11812: (1992). Problems with chiefdoms: Pauketat (2007).
11813: 
11814: My concluding comments on phenomenology and cultural evolution share
11815: ground with some elements of Whitley (2002), particularly his
11816: identification of ‘ancestors’ with processual and Marxist models.
11817: 
11818: Chapter 10 Archaeology and Darwinian Evolution
11819: 
11820: As I also feel with strands of theory such as Marxism, it is advisable
11821: for the student to grasp something of the structure of Darwinian thought
11822: before asking how to apply it to archaeology. For an introduction to
11823: Darwin and discussion of recent Darwinian thought, the two major
11824: thinkers are Steven Jay Gould (1989, 1997, 2002) and Richard Dawkins
11825: (1989). Both write accessibly and have been extremely influential; Gould
11826: in particular has stressed the role of contingency and historical
11827: accident in evolution, and has played an indirect role in the
11828: ‘softening’ of many evolutionary models in archaeology. I would add
11829: Richerson and Boyd (2005) as an accessible and compelling account of
11830: coevolutionary theory. The subtitle ‘Darwin’s dangerous idea’ is taken
11831: from Dennett (1995).
11832: 
11833: Shennan 2008 is a good bibliographic review of the archaeological
11834: literature, complemented for North America by Clark (2003). Shennan
11835: (2002) is an important statement of a different approach to evolution –
11836: note that there is almost no reference to the ‘Dunnell school’ in this
11837: book. See also the critique of Shennan in Kristiansen (2004).
11838: 
11839: The style-function dichotomy: Brantingham (2007). Bentley and Shennan
11840: (2003) is a good case study in Darwinian approaches to cultural
11841: transmission. Costly signalling theory: McGuire and Hildebrandt (2005).
11842: See also subsequent exchanges in American Antiquity 72:2.
11843: 
11844: Selectionist archaeology: O’Brien (ed.) (1996) is a good place to start,
11845: and includes much of Dunnell’s writings. See also Dunnell (1980, 1989);
11846: Rindos (1989). Teltser (1995) is a collection of papers dealing with
11847: methodological issues raised by selectionist approaches. Barton and
11848: Clark (1997) contains some good papers. In particular, Fraser Neiman is
11849: a lucid and enthusiastic proponent of such models (1997). Other recent
11850: collections include Maschner (1996), O’Brien (1996), Arnold (1996). For
11851: the argument that culture history came close to a Darwinian approach,
11852: see Lyman and O’Brien (1997a, 2006).
11853: 
11854: Case studies: Falk (1997) on the brain. Arnold et al. (1997) versus Raab
11855: and Larson (1997): two contrasting views of the interpretation of the
11856: same set of environmental data. Larson et al. (1996): compare with
11857: McGuire and Saitta (1996), both parties working on similar material.
11858: 
11859: Fabian (1998) is an edited volume of accessible articles that gives a
11860: good idea of the very wide variation in contemporary evolutionary
11861: thought. Ridley (1997) is a good introductory ‘reader’. For a critical
11862: approach see Midgley (1985) and Giddens (1984).
11863: 
11864: Optimal foraging theory: see Mithen for an individual-based approach to
11865: cultural ecology; also the debate between Mithen and Thomas (Thomas
11866: 1988, 1991; Mithen 1991). Mithen (1989) on ecological approaches to art;
11867: compare with other studies in the same volume. Risk: Halstead and O’Shea
11868: (1989).
11869: 
11870: Case studies: Gamble (2007) is to my mind the best in its field. Larson
11871: and Michaelsen (1990) and Larson et al. (1996) see Anasazi cultural
11872: development as a response to population growth and climatic
11873: deterioration; see reply by Allison (1996) focusing on whether their
11874: chronology works. Contrast also with McGuire and Saitta in the same
11875: volume. A variety of work balances ‘social’ and ‘ecological’ approaches
11876: in a consideration of environment; for example, Hastorf and Johanessen
11877: (1991) and Schortman and Urban (1992). See also studies in Crumley
11878: (1994). ‘Sexy handaxe theory’: Kohn and Mithen (1999); Machin (2008);
11879: Mithen (2008). Neiman (2008) is a fascinating study from historical
11880: archaeology.
11881: 
11882: For the nineteenth-century cultural context of Darwin, see Beer (1996)
11883: and Smith (2009).
11884: 
11885: Schiffer (1996) argues for a rapprochement between behavioural and
11886: evolutionary archaeology.
11887: 
11888: Chapter 11 Archaeology and History
11889: 
11890: Current approaches to Classical archaeology: Morris (2004), Alcock and
11891: Osborne (2007). Hingley (2005) looks at Roman archaeology.
11892: 
11893: Moreland (2006) is an authoritative summary of current thinking on the
11894: relationship between archaeology and texts; see also Moreland (2001) and
11895: Tabaczyñski (1993). O’Keeffe (2007) discusses the relationship between
11896: archaeology and art history; in Johnson 2007, I discuss the relationship
11897: between archaeology and landscape history. Special Issues of
11898: Archaeologia Polona 44 (2006) has a series of papers on the relationship
11899: between archaeology, anthropology and history. Malina and Vasicek (1990)
11900: is a powerful statement of archaeology as a historical discipline, and a
11901: useful summary in English of many of the concerns of central and eastern
11902: European archaeology.
11903: 
11904: Early medieval Europe: Hills (2003), Halsall (1997), Staecker (2005) and
11905: comments in the next issue, Hedeagar (2006) and responses in succeeding
11906: issue.
11907: 
11908: Annales school: Le Goff and Nora (1985) is a collection of varied
11909: papers, ranging from (early) work on climate, demography and
11910: quantitative methods to (later) studies of ideologies and mental
11911: structures. Braudel (1980) is a collection of his papers; see also Le
11912: Roy Ladurie (1974, 1978). Annales archaeology: Knapp (1992) is the best
11913: collection of Annales-influenced papers, the best of which is Duke
11914: (1992), though Moreland’s paper in this volume argues convincingly
11915: against the utility of Annales thinking for archaeology. Chartier (1994)
11916: is the best example of his work.
11917: 
11918: The ‘linguistic turn’: Joyce (1995); Easthope (1993). The work of Hayden
11919: White (1987) and Dominic LaCapra (1987) has been central in the critique
11920: of history as a narrative: see Jenkins (1995, 1997). Hunt (1989) is a
11921: useful introduction to the New Cultural History. For a critique, Evans
11922: (2nd edn 2000) is a passionately written, influential, lucid
11923: condemnation of postmodern forms of ‘theory’ in history. The responses
11924: of Evans’s critics, and his response in turn, have been gathered
11925: together by the Institute of Historical Research
11926: (http://www.history.ac.uk/discourse/index.xhtml, accessed 10.10.2008).
11927: 
11928: ‘Processual’ approaches to historical archaeology include South
11929: (1977a,b), Binford (1977). Brumfiel (2003) explores the interfaces
11930: between history, archaeology and anthropology within North American
11931: traditions; see also Deagan (1991). Deagan (1983) for the St Augustine
11932: project: an excellent case study is Voss’ (2008b) reformulation of
11933: gender, race and labour.
11934: 
11935: Deetz’s work, particularly his application of structuralism to
11936: historical archaeology, has had a huge influence. His simply written yet
11937: theoretically sophisticated application of structuralism to historical
11938: archaeology, In Small Things Forgotten (1977), remains my favourite
11939: archaeology book. See also Deetz (1988) and Yentsch and Beaudry (1992).
11940: 
11941: The historical archaeology of capitalism is a huge field. A few
11942: introductory references are Leone (2005); Orser (1996); Johnson (1996);
11943: Delle (1998).
11944: 
11945: ‘Contact period’ studies embody a wide range of approaches: compare
11946: Leonard (1993) with Turnbaugh (1993) in the same volume. Contrasts
11947: between colonial and Indigenous interpretations are discussed in Anawalt
11948: (1996) and Crowell (1997). Oral traditions and ethnohistory: Damm (2005)
11949: and comments in succeeding issues. Case studies: Bergman (2006) relates
11950: archaeology to Saami ethnohistory; see response by Knutson (2007).
11951: Hrobat (2007) looks at oral tradition, memory and landscape. David et
11952: al. (2002) is a model case study.
11953: 
11954: Case studies of historical archaeology: Little (2007) is a good place to
11955: start. Then Leone (2005) and Voss (2008c). McGuire and Paynter (1991),
11956: especially the paper by Yentsch; Kepecs and Kolb (1997); and Tarlow and
11957: West (1998). Yentsch (1994) is a classic study of historical
11958: archaeology. Lightfoot et al. (1998) is a fascinating study of practices
11959: and identities. Gilchrist (1993) is a study of medieval nunneries. See
11960: also Wall (1994), Spector (1993).
11961: 
11962: African historical archaeology: Schmidt and Walz (2007); Hall (2000).
11963: Schrire (1995) is a powerful and moving account of historical
11964: archaeology in South Africa, for which see also Lucas and Hall (2006).
11965: 
11966: Bodiam Castle: Coulson (1992), Johnson (2002). Ronnes (1999), Hansson
11967: (2006) and Virágos discuss comparable issues for other areas of Europe.
11968: Platt (2007) is a restatement of traditional views; make your own mind
11969: up.
11970: 
11971: Chapter 12 Archaeology, Politics and Culture
11972: 
11973: The postmodern condition: Lyotard (1984). Lyotard, rarely among
11974: postmodern thinkers, writes in an accessible and elegant way. Rorty
11975: (1989) also writes accessibly. Harvey (1989) is a classic review and
11976: critique of postmodernism from a Marxist perspective. See also Rorty and
11977: Engel (2007), and Hollinger (1994). Postmodernism in other disciplines:
11978: Jencks (1977: architecture), Gregory (1994: geography), Jenkins (1995,
11979: 1997), White (1987) and Hunt (1989: history; Evans (2000) for critique).
11980: No discussion of postmodernism would be complete without a discussion of
11981: the ‘Sokal hoax’: see Sokal and Bricmont (1998), and Sokal (2008).
11982: Ashman and Baringer (2000) contains a variety of responses to the hoax.
11983: 
11984: Foucault has an undeserved reputation as a difficult writer. It’s best
11985: to take him on his own terms; he frequently writes in a rhetorical
11986: rather than analytical style, and frequently uses extended metaphors
11987: (most famously that of archaeology itself). Again, Foucault refuses to
11988: be pinned down to a set of concrete causes or a cut-and-dried ‘model’.
11989: Read some of his books on tangible issues such as Madness and
11990: Civilisation and Discipline and Punish before moving on to his more
11991: methodological works such as An Archaeology of Knowledge and The Order
11992: of Things.
11993: 
11994: Archaeology and politics: parts VII and VII of Preucel and Hodder
11995: (1996), especially Gero and Root and Trigger. Fawcett et al. (eds)
11996: (2008) contains a range of recent work. See also Hinsley (1989). Eller
11997: (1997) is a concise and lucid general introduction to the issues around
11998: ‘multiculturalism’. Bender (1998) is a passionate account of the
11999: politics of Stonehenge. Jones (1997) is a fascinating account of past
12000: interpretations of Chichen Itza. McGuire (2008) is a restatement of a
12001: Marxist view and a fascinating case study.
12002: 
12003: Archaeology and Indigenous knowledge: start with Atalay (2006) and
12004: Lippert (2006). Wilcox (2010) is an important discussion of NAGPRA which
12005: has influenced my presentation of the issues here. Tuhiwai Smith (1999)
12006: explores background issues. Watkins (2000) concentrates on
12007: ethical/legislative issues; see also Watkins (2003). Ferguson (1996) is
12008: a review of the literature on Native Americans and archaeology, now a
12009: little out of date. Kennewick Man: Thomas (2000), Burke et al. (eds)
12010: (2008). Lippert (2005) is an important move beyond simplistic and
12011: polarized issues of ‘ownership’. López Austin and López Luján (2001) is
12012: a powerful account of Mexico’s Indigenous past. Conkey (2005) discusses
12013: the intersection between feminist and Indigenous archaeologies: see also
12014: ensuing discussion. Indigenous archaeology in Australia: McNiven and
12015: Russell (2005), who recommend ‘partnership research’ beyond community
12016: archaeology, and the archaeology of oral tradition, as the way forward.
12017: McGhee (2008) is a controversial rebuttal of ‘Indigenous archaeology’;
12018: as I write responses in future issues of American Antiquity are
12019: forthcoming. Blain and Wallis (2004, 2007) on modern Pagans.
12020: 
12021: Nationalism, for obvious reasons, is a major preoccupation with European
12022: archaeologists: Hamilakis and Yalouri (1996); Atkinson et al. (1996).
12023: Diaz-Andreu and Champion (1996) and Kohl and Fawcett (1996) are good
12024: collections of case studies, though see Hamilakis (1996) and Lampeter
12025: Archaeology Workshop (1997) for an effective critique of the latter.
12026: Cooper et al. (1995) and Walsh (1992) discuss the theoretical basis of
12027: ‘heritage management’ and cultural identity in Britain. Curta (2001) and
12028: Piontek (2006) discuss archaeology and Slavic identity; Piotrowska
12029: (1997-8) is an important discussion of Biskupin. Hamilakis (2007) is an
12030: important study, moving beyond an exclusive concern with nationalism.
12031: Nationalism and archaeology in Iran: Abdi (2001). Zimbabwe: Fontein
12032: (2006). Abu El-Haj (2001) is a scholarly and important study of Israeli
12033: archaeology.
12034: 
12035: Archaeology under the Nazis: Arnold (1990) and Arnold and Hassmann
12036: (1995); Härke and Wolfram (1993); Jones et al. (1996); Schulke (2000);
12037: Legendre et al. (2007). Diaz-Andreu (1993) on archaeology under Franco.
12038: 
12039: Community archaeology: Marshall (ed.) (2002) is an important collection
12040: of papers, especially Moser et al. (2002). The Annapolis Project is an
12041: excellent case study in building a critical public awareness. See Leone
12042: (2005); also Potter (1994). See also Sabloff (2008). Schmidt (2001)
12043: discusses theoretical issues in museums interpretation and compares the
12044: Anglo scene with Germany
12045: 
12046: Postcolonial issues: Gosden (2001); Chakrabarti (1998) on India; Andah
12047: (1995), Hall (2000) and Shepherd (2002) on Africa. Cultural hybridity:
12048: Falck (2003). Wilson (1999) is a fascinating series of studies of
12049: cultural contact. Hingley (2005) is a postcolonial view of Roman
12050: archaeology. Silliman (2005) discusses native North American archaeology
12051: and argues for seeing it the period as ‘colonial’ rather than one of
12052: ‘contact’. Harrison (2003) explores mimesis in knapped bottle glass
12053: artefacts. India: Chakrabarti (2003), Pratap (2009). The Americas:
12054: Bernal (1980); Lumbreras (2005); Politis (2001, 2002); Politis and
12055: Alberti (1999); various in Funari et al. (eds) (2005). Scheinsohn (2003)
12056: argues that the dominance of North American scholarship has obscured
12057: South American diversity with regard to the archaeological record for
12058: hunter-gathers.
12059: 
12060: African burial ground: the National Parks Service website has a brief
12061: account and links to the final reports
12062: (http://www.nps.gov/afbg/historyculture/index.htm). On wider issues of
12063: African-American archaeology, see Ferguson (1992), Blakey (1995) and
12064: Singleton (1985, 1995, and references therein). On wider issues of the
12065: misuse of genetics, see Pluciennik (1996); Mirza and Dungworth (1995).
12066: 
12067: Relativism: Trigger (1989); Wylie (1992b); Brumfiel (1996). Fotiadis
12068: (1994) is a restatement that this claimed objectivity is illusory. Davis
12069: (1992), Gosden (1992) and Saitta (1992) offer thoughtful discussion of
12070: related aspects of this debate. Lampeter Archaeology Workshop (1997)
12071: offers a critical response.
12072: 
12073: World Archaeological Congress and social justice issues: Smith and Ward
12074: (2000) discuss the social background. Ucko (1989) is entertaining, but
12075: should be read with critical caution.
12076: 
12077: Issues of representation and performativity: Shanks (1992); Shanks and
12078: Pearson (2001). Moser (1996) discusses issues of visual representation.
12079: 
12080: Chapter 13 Conclusion: The Future of Theory
12081: 
12082: It is worth comparing consensual ‘position statements’ on aims and
12083: priorities for archaeology, for example that of Nordic TAG
12084: (http://www.aal.au.dk/nt/enkeltesessions/priorities, accessed
12085: 10.10.2008), the Archaeology Benchmarking Statement laying out a
12086: ‘benchmark’ for undergraduate teaching in the UK
12087: (http://www.qaa.ac.uk/academicinfrastructure/benchmark/statements/Archaeology.asp,
12088: accessed 10.10.2008), and its Australian counterpart
12089: (www.australianarchaeologicalassociation.com.au/files/by_degrees.pdf,
12090: accessed 23.08.2009). Critical comment on an earlier version of the UK
12091: Benchmarking statement can be found in Rainbird and Hamilakis (2001).
12092: Patrik’s (1985) influential article argues for the coexistence of
12093: different schools of thought centred around different definitions of
12094: what constitutes the archaeological record.
12095: 
12096: The current state: an extremely useful volume is Holtorf and Karlsson
12097: (2000), which in its use of commentaries and replies by the author gives
12098: a good flavour of diversity across the globe. Funari et al. (eds) (2005)
12099: and Kobyliñski (ed.) (2001) are also useful in this context. ‘World
12100: theory’: Schmidt and Patterson (1995); Ucko (1995); issues of
12101: Archaeologies, published by the World Archaeological Congress.
12102: 
12103: Reflexive approaches to fieldwork: Berggrren and Hodder (2003); Chadwick
12104: (2003); Lucas (2001); Yarrow (2003); Holtorf (2002); Moser (2007). Much
12105: of this literature also contributes to the study of materiality and
12106: artfeact biography. See also the extensive literature on the Çatalhöyük
12107: project, including Hodder (1999) and http://www.catalhoyuk.com/.
12108: Tomáskóva (2007) explores field practices, particularly mapmaking, from
12109: a feminist standpoint.
12110: 
12111: Materiality: the proposition that things create people as much as people
12112: create things is set out most clearly by Gosden (2005). For a range of
12113: approaches, see Skibo and Schiffer (2008), DeMarrais et al. (eds)
12114: (2004), Brumfiel (2003), Knappett (2005), Meskell (2004), Meskell (ed.)
12115: (2005), Olsen (2003). Herva and Ikäheimo (2002) on rock art and
12116: materiality.
12117: 
12118: Symmetrical archaeology: Latour has been especially influential; I find
12119: his case studies (for example the Berliner Key essay in Graves-Brown
12120: (ed.) 2000 and the first part of Latour 1987) more compelling than his
12121: more general arguments, for example Latour (2005). Webmoor and Witmore
12122: (2008) set out the case for a critique of ‘the social’, drawing on Gell
12123: (1992, 1998) and Latour (2005). Witmore (2006); Webmoor (2007);
12124: Gonzáles-Ruibal (2006) and responses in succeeding issues; papers in
12125: Gonzáles-Ruibal (ed.) (2007); papers in World Archaeology 39:4.
12126: 
12127: Memory: Jan Assman’s work (2006) is especially important. Van Dyke and
12128: Alcock (eds) (2003), Jones (2007), Campbell (2006) for a Polynesian case
12129: study. Temporality: Lucas (2005), Harding (2005).
12130:
